message.content:
Review the plan for goal **G10** (observability & metrics accuracy) in CONFIGURED mode (return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Read `fetch_item("goals","G10")`, its 3 defects (D48/D52/D53), and tasks T133 (D48) → T134 (D53, deps T133), T135 (D52 parallel) under M46.

Be adversarial per the /cq:plan-review rubric. Ground against internal/bind/probe.go, internal/bind/multipath.go (txBytes/rxBytes sites, echo reflection, NewMultipath signature + struct), internal/bind/pathsock.go (listenPath swallows the listenOnDevice error), internal/device/device.go (reloadWarnings + NewMultipath call sites), internal/metrics, config.Config (Scheduler/FEC/DNS/Bind, where BindMode lives). Check: (1) D48 — does counting probe+echo into txBytes actually fix the path_up=1/tx=0 idle-standby symptom without double-counting or a locking hazard; is the T104 subtest flip correctly identified? (2) D53 — is returning the fallback-fact+error from listenPath (keeping pathsock.go logging-free) actually cleaner than threading a logger into pathsock.go, and does the plan WARN at BOTH the unresolvable-interface AND setsockopt layers incl. the pre-existing CAP fallback; are ALL NewMultipath callers (device.go + bind tests) covered? (3) D52 — is the zeroed-copy catch-all sound (must NOT warn on Metrics/path-membership which ARE applied), and is the Bind-per-path-vs-top-level question resolvable by the implementer? (4) serialization (T134 after T133 on multipath.go) necessary; T135 genuinely parallel? (5) Missing scope (e.g. docs-sync).

Return JSON ONLY: {"summary": "...", "verdict": "go-ahead"|"revise", "new_questions": [], "criticism": []}. End with a `### Session summary` block.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: {"item":{"id":"D48","milestoneId":"M30","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total omits probe and echo-reflection wire bytes (tx/rx accounting asymmetry behind path_up=1/tx=0)","description":"Both T104 reviewers ([opus]+[fable]) independently source-confirmed: internal/bind/probe.go emitProbes (probe.go:44) writes each PROBE frame via conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort WITHOUT incrementing ps.txBytes, and dispatchInbound's echo reflection (multipath.go ~1284) is likewise uncounted. ps.txBytes.Add exists at exactly two sites — Send (multipath.go:1477) and fecFlushDeadline (:1603) — both DATA/PARITY paths. Meanwhile rxBytes counts EVERY inbound datagram (DATA, PROBE, echo — multipath.go:881) and the metric help string claims 'Total bytes transmitted on the path.' The asymmetry makes a healthy idle standby (active-backup collapses all DATA onto the primary) read path_up=1 with tx=0 while rx grows — exactly the G6/I8-motivating production observation. [fable] additionally empirically validated the T104 repro fixture (BlockEgress one-way tc-clsact block coexisting with netem). This is a METRICS-ACCOUNTING fault, NOT a liveness hole: liveness is genuinely bidirectional (only HandleEcho on our own probe's authenticated echo marks up; an egress-dead standby goes DOWN within DownAfter). The T104 subtest 'standby-transmits-when-idle' (test/e2e/standby_liveness_test.go) is the ready-made repro, predicted (source-consistent) to FAIL against current code until fixed.","severity":"medium","suggestedFix":"In the fix task decide the counter contract: either count probe emission (emitProbes) + echo reflection into ps.txBytes so tx matches rx's true-wire-volume semantics (optionally add a separate DATA-only series for the data-thrift signal the doc at multipath.go:140-151 describes), or re-document/rename the metric so its help string stops claiming total transmitted bytes. Then flip T104's standby-transmits-when-idle subtest from repro-failure to the green acceptance check.","ledgerRefs":["tasks:T104","goals:G6"],"rootCause":"CONFIRMED as filed (both T104 reviewers source-confirmed). ps.txBytes.Add fires only at the two DATA/PARITY sites (Send multipath.go:1477, fecFlushDeadline :1603); PROBE emission (probe.go:44 emitProbes) and echo reflection (multipath.go ~1284) are uncounted, while rxBytes counts every inbound datagram — so a healthy idle standby reads path_up=1 with tx=0 while rx grows (the I8-motivating observation). A metrics-accounting fault, NOT a liveness hole. File-and-deferred to goal G10 (count probe/echo into txBytes or re-document the metric; flip T104's standby-transmits-when-idle subtest to green)."},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T03:18:22.478Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T09:10:19.050Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}}

content.text: {"item":{"id":"D52","milestoneId":"M32","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"reloadWarnings omits scheduler/fec/dns/bind non-path config sections (SIGHUP silently ignores them)","description":"Filed from T109 round-1 review ([fable], file-and-defer per K13). internal/device/device.go reloadWarnings compares role/psk/wireguard/amnezia/log + path params/order, but Config also carries Scheduler, FEC, DNS, and Bind sections — a SIGHUP changing any of those is silently ignored with NO warning, contradicting Reload's documented invariant that every ignored non-path change must produce an explicit warning ('SILENCE is not acceptable'). Pre-existing at base 1fd915f (those fields were added after T30 without extending reloadWarnings). NOTE: the [bind] section here is T105's new BindMode; [dns] is G5's DNS block. Out of scope for T109 (which only adds the analogous warning for its own tun_persist field).","severity":"medium","suggestedFix":"Extend reloadWarnings with reflect.DeepEqual comparisons for Scheduler, FEC, DNS, and Bind (mirroring the existing wireguard/amnezia/log cases) + unit-test cases; OR compare a struct copy with the path/metrics fields zeroed so the warning set is future-proof against new Config fields.","ledgerRefs":["tasks:T109","goals:G6"],"rootCause":"CONFIRMED as filed ([fable] T109 review). internal/device/device.go reloadWarnings compares role/psk/wireguard/amnezia/log + path params but omits the Scheduler, FEC, DNS, and Bind sections — a SIGHUP changing any of those is silently ignored, contradicting Reload's documented 'SILENCE is not acceptable' invariant. File-and-deferred to goal G10 (extend reloadWarnings with DeepEqual over Scheduler/FEC/DNS/Bind, or a zeroed-copy comparison future-proof against new Config fields)."},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T04:01:04.560Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T09:10:23.050Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}}

content.text: {"item":{"id":"D53","milestoneId":"M31","status":"root-caused","fields":{"headline":"Device-bind fallback to source-IP pinning is silent (no WARN) in internal/bind","description":"Both T106 reviewers ([opus]+[fable]) independently filed this. An operator who explicitly sets bind=\"device\" can silently end up source-IP-pinned — either an unresolvable interface in selectDeviceBinds/resolveForcedDeviceBind, OR a failed SO_BINDTODEVICE setsockopt in listenPath — losing the roam-survival property the mode was chosen for, with NO log signal. internal/bind holds no logger (NewMultipath, multipath.go:514, takes no logger param) and the PRE-EXISTING CAP/setsockopt fallback in listenPath is itself silent, so T106's 'matching the existing CAP fallback' is factually satisfied by silence. Fixing requires threading a logger through NewMultipath — broader than T106's selection-logic scope, so filed-and-deferred.","severity":"medium","suggestedFix":"Thread the repo's internal/log logger through NewMultipath into listenPath/planPathBinds/resolveForcedDeviceBind and WARN on every forced-device fallback to source-IP binding (both the unresolvable-interface and the setsockopt-failure layers; covers the pre-existing CAP fallback too), naming the path + interface.","ledgerRefs":["tasks:T106","goals:G6"],"rootCause":"CONFIRMED as filed (both T106 reviewers). An operator setting bind=\"device\" can silently end up source-IP-pinned (unresolvable interface in selectDeviceBinds/resolveForcedDeviceBind, OR a failed SO_BINDTODEVICE setsockopt in listenPath) with NO log signal — internal/bind holds no logger (NewMultipath takes none) and the pre-existing CAP/setsockopt fallback is itself silent. File-and-deferred to goal G10 (thread internal/log through NewMultipath into listenPath/planPathBinds/resolveForcedDeviceBind and WARN on every forced-device→source fallback, naming path+interface)."},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T04:18:15.521Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T09:10:27.605Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}}

content.text: {"items":{"tasks":[{"id":"T133","milestoneId":"M46","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Count probe emission + echo-reflection wire bytes into ps.txBytes and flip the T104 standby-idle subtest green (D48)","description":"wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total under-reports because ps.txBytes is charged only on the DATA/PARITY send paths (Send + fecFlushDeadline), while rxBytes counts EVERY inbound datagram — so a healthy idle standby (active-backup collapses DATA onto the primary) reads path_up=1 with tx=0 while rx grows (the I8-motivating observation). Adopt the true-wire-volume contract (D48's preferred option): (1) internal/bind/probe.go emitProbes (~:50): after a successful ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(raw, remote), add ps.txBytes.Add(uint64(len(raw))) — ONLY on a nil error, mirroring how rxBytes counts bytes actually pulled off the socket. (2) internal/bind/multipath.go dispatchInbound echo reflection (~:1693, line refs drifted — locate the echo WriteToUDPAddrPort): after a successful write add ps.txBytes.Add(uint64(len(echo))). Both sites use the atomic counter outside m.mu — no locking change. (3) Verify the wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total help string (internal/metrics) is now ACCURATE ('Total bytes transmitted on the path' — adjust only if it still implies DATA-only); do NOT add a separate DATA-only series (out of scope). (4) NOTE: throughput derivation (internal/device/metrics.go) sums tx+rx deltas, so counted probe bytes slightly raise reported idle throughput — intended wire-volume semantics. FIRST bind task — T134 (D53) serializes after it (both edit multipath.go).","acceptance":"`go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/bind/... ./internal/metrics/...` pass; new unit tests (probe_test.go fake-clock pattern) assert ps.txBytes increments by the emitted frame length for (a) emitProbes probe emission and (b) an inbound PROBE's echo reflection, and FAIL against the unpatched code. T104's 'standby-transmits-when-idle' subtest (test/e2e/standby_liveness_test.go) is flipped from expected-repro-failure to the GREEN acceptance check (idle standby shows tx_bytes>0 while path_up=1); compiles under -tags e2e (privileged run per the o3/llm-ubuntu-0 procedure).","suggestedModel":"standard","ledgerRefs":["goals:G10","defects:D48"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T10:07:27.505Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T10:07:27.505Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"},{"id":"T134","milestoneId":"M46","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Thread internal/log through NewMultipath and WARN on every forced-device-bind fallback to source-IP pinning (D53)","description":"internal/bind holds no logger, so the SO_BINDTODEVICE→source-IP fallback is SILENT — an operator setting bind=\"device\" can end up source-IP-pinned (losing roam survival) with no signal (D53). (1) Add a log.Logger parameter to NewMultipath (internal/bind/multipath.go, ~:549/612) and store it component-scoped (logger.Component(\"bind\")); update ALL call sites: the ~9 in internal/device/device.go (device already holds t.log) + the ~6 internal/bind test files (a discard-writer log for tests). Fail fast on a nil logger, consistent with the constructor's existing nil-checks. (2) WARN at BOTH fallback layers, naming path + interface: (a) unresolvable-interface layer — where planPathBinds/resolveForcedDeviceBind (internal/bind/pathsock.go) yields dev==\"\" for a path whose resolved mode is BindModeDevice; (b) setsockopt-failure layer — listenPath (pathsock.go:35) currently SWALLOWS the listenOnDevice error: restructure so the caller can log it — PREFERRED (keeps pathsock.go logging-free) is to RETURN the fallback fact + underlying error alongside the conn and WARN at the call sites (Open, AddPath via the addPathListen seam, reconcile.go). This covers the PRE-EXISTING silent CAP/setsockopt fallback too; distinguish FORCED (bind=\"device\", operator-chosen roam property lost — WARN) from AUTO (informational). Keep the m.resolveDeviceBind/m.addPathListen function-field seams working for tests. Docs-sync per AGENTS.md if bind-fallback behaviour is documented. Serialized after T133 (both edit multipath.go; T134 changes the NewMultipath signature + struct).","acceptance":"`go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/bind/... ./internal/device/...` pass (all NewMultipath call sites updated); new unit tests inject a capturing log.Logger and assert ONE WARN naming path+interface for (a) an unresolvable forced device (non-existent interface) and (b) a failing setsockopt fallback (driven via the addPathListen/deferredListen seams), and NO WARN on a successful device bind or a source-mode path; the WARN-on-fallback tests FAIL against the unpatched (silent) code; the fallback still returns a working source-IP-bound socket.","suggestedModel":"standard","dependsOn":["T133"],"ledgerRefs":["goals:G10","defects:D53"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T10:07:46.968Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T10:07:46.968Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"},{"id":"T135","milestoneId":"M46","status":"planned","fields":{"headline":"Extend reloadWarnings to cover the Scheduler/FEC/DNS/Bind reload-immutable config sections (D52)","description":"reloadWarnings (internal/device/device.go ~:549) reports a change for Role/PSK/WireGuard/Amnezia/Log/TUNPersist + same-name path source/dest + reorder, but OMITS the Scheduler, FEC, DNS, and Bind sections — a SIGHUP changing any of those is silently accepted while the running tunnel keeps the booted values, contradicting Reload's documented 'SILENCE is not acceptable' invariant (D52). Fix: (1) add reflect.DeepEqual comparisons with actionable per-section messages for the top-level Scheduler, FEC, and DNS sections (mirroring the WireGuard/Amnezia/Log cases). (2) For Bind (T105's BindMode): DETERMINE where it lives in config.Config — if per-path (config.Path.Bind), extend the existing same-name-path comparison (currently SourceAddr/DestAddr only) to also warn when l.Bind != d.Bind ('path %q bind mode changed — the running socket keeps its original binding'); if a top-level section, add a DeepEqual case. (3) FUTURE-PROOF (D52 suggestedFix option B): add a final catch-all comparing struct copies with ALL handled fields zeroed (Paths, Metrics — which IS applied, so must NOT warn — and every individually-compared/warned field) via reflect.DeepEqual, warning generically that other config sections changed — so a future Config field can never silently regress this invariant. Do NOT warn on Metrics (Reload applies it) or on path membership add/remove (applied). Keep reloadWarnings a pure function. Docs-sync design.md reload section if it enumerates warned sections. INDEPENDENT of the bind tasks (device-only, parallel).","acceptance":"`go build ./... && go test ./internal/device/...` pass; new table-driven internal/device/reload_test.go cases each mutate exactly one of Scheduler/FEC/DNS/Bind between live and desired and assert reloadWarnings returns exactly one corresponding warning; a case mutating Metrics asserts NO warning (still applied); a synthetic added-but-unhandled field is caught by the zeroed-copy catch-all (or its coverage documented); existing warning cases stay green.","suggestedModel":"standard","ledgerRefs":["goals:G10","defects:D52"]},"createdAt":"2026-07-14T10:07:57.865Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-14T10:07:57.865Z","author":"fable-5","session":"671d5adc-7e2a-440e-b87d-6da40edeb7b7"}]}}

It is NOT a summary, outline, or stale cache. Treat each block as a Read you have already performed: do not Read a file shown here.

**`internal/bind/multipath.go`** — Load(calls), peerPathState(references), offer(calls), observeRecovered(calls), handleInbound(method), Decode(calls), +9 more

```go
1359	// here — PARITY (dropped today) will feed the decoder, which reconstructs missing
1360	// DATA frames and calls Observe with their ORIGINAL outer-seq, identical to a
1361	// natively-received frame.
1362	func (m *Multipath) handleInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1363		fr, err := ps.codec.Decode(raw)
1364		if err != nil {
1365			return // drop malformed / PSK-mismatched outer frames
1366		}
1367		m.dispatchInbound(ps, fr, raw, srcAP)
1368	}
1369	
1370	// demuxInbound is the readLoop's per-frame entry on a (possibly shared) socket: it resolves
1371	// which bound peer's view owns the datagram, then hands the frame to handleInbound on THAT

... (gap) ...

1616	// peer's stream against that peer's own buffer; on the single-peer edge/hub ps.peer is the
1617	// embedded primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split singleton. raw is retained for
1618	// the probe transport (HandleEcho / Reflect re-decode it under the peer's psk).
1619	func (m *Multipath) dispatchInbound(ps *peerPathState, fr frame.Frame, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1620		pr := ps.peer
1621		switch f := fr.(type) {
1622		case frame.Data:
1623			// Decode already returned a fresh copy of the payload (it aliases nothing
1624			// else), so the resequencer may take ownership of it directly.
1625			rq := pr.resequencer.Load()
1626			if rq == nil {
1627				// The peer's heavy receive state is absent — not yet instantiated, or torn down on
1628				// session/liveness loss while a DATA frame was in flight on this readLoop. Drop it;
1629				// a fresh authenticated PROBE re-instantiates the ring before the next DATA lands.
1630				return
1631			}
1632			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1633				// FEC on (T24): offer the data shard to the decoder BEFORE resequencing so a
1634				// later parity frame can reconstruct any group-mate lost in transit, then
1635				// deliver THIS received frame in its own right (the decoder never echoes a
1636				// directly-received data shard back). The shard's coded bytes are
1637				// OuterSeq || Payload — the same bytes the sender coded parity over.
1638				shard := fec.DataShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.FECIndex), Payload: fecShardPayload(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload)}
1639				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1640				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1641				// Residual-loss accounting (T29): this outer-seq was natively delivered, so mark
1642				// it present in the post-recovery loss estimator. A seq never marked here nor via
1643				// a reconstruction below is loss that FEC did not mask.
1644				if fr.connLoss != nil {
1645					fr.connLoss.Observe(f.OuterSeq)
1646				}
1647				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
1648			} else {
1649				rq.Observe(f.OuterSeq, f.Payload, srcAP)
1650			}
1651		case frame.Parity:
1652			// PARITY feeds the FEC decoder (T24); a group that has now accumulated enough
1653			// shards reconstructs its missing data frames, each resequenced at its ORIGINAL
1654			// outer-seq (carried in the recovered shard's coded bytes) so recovery composes
1655			// with T18 exactly like a natively-received frame. With FEC off, PARITY is
1656			// dropped (the pre-T24 behaviour).
1657			if fr := pr.fecRecv.Load(); fr != nil {
1658				rq := pr.resequencer.Load()
1659				if rq == nil {
1660					return // heavy receive state torn down mid-flight (see the DATA case)
1661				}
1662				shard := fec.ParityShard{Group: fec.GroupID(f.FECGroup), Index: int(f.ParityIndex), DataCount: int(f.DataCount), Payload: f.Payload}
1663				recovered, _ := fr.offer(shard)
1664				m.observeRecovered(fr, rq, recovered, srcAP)
1665			}
1666		case frame.Probe:
1667			// Authenticated (the PROBE MAC verified in Decode): learn the return remote
1668			// from it, below the engine's virtual endpoint (no roaming churn).
1669			ps.setRemote(srcAP)
1670			if f.IsEcho {
1671				if ps.prober != nil {
1672					// A replay/forgery/wrong-path echo is rejected inside HandleEcho and
1673					// leaves liveness untouched; the error is a per-frame drop, not fatal.
1674					// ps.prober is the path's OWN immutable prober — never a lookup into a
1675					// dynamically-mutated slice — so runtime add/remove cannot race this.
1676					_ = ps.prober.HandleEcho(raw)
1677					// Sticky "ever had a live path" latch (I4): a fresh echo that just brought
1678					// this path to StateUp (or found it already Up) flips everUp permanently.
1679					// Checked here rather than only in Liveness.transition so the bind-level
1680					// predicate needs no wiring through NewProber/NewLiveness — it observes the
1681					// SAME state HandleEcho just updated, at the one call site that can ever
1682					// change Down->Up.
1683					if ps.prober.State() == telemetry.StateUp {
1684						m.everUp.Store(true)
1685					}
1686				}
1687				return
1688			}
1689			if pr.reflector != nil {
```

```go
10	// emitProbes performs one probe cadence step: for every currently-open path it
11	// emits one authenticated PROBE frame (IsEcho=false) to that path's learned/
12	// configured remote, then Ticks that path's Prober so liveness advances against
13	// the injected clock. A path without a known remote yet is still Ticked (so a
14	// silent path is detected Down) but nothing is sent — there is nowhere to send.
15	//
16	// Concurrency mirrors Send: the path/prober set is snapshotted under m.mu, then
17	// released before any socket I/O, so emission neither holds the lock across a
18	// syscall nor blocks the lock-free receive fast path. A concurrent Close closes
19	// the sockets out from under the snapshot; the resulting write error is benign
20	// (teardown) and dropped. It is a no-op when the bind has no probers or is closed.
21	func (m *Multipath) emitProbes() {
22		m.mu.Lock()
23		if len(m.paths) == 0 || m.probers == nil {
24			m.mu.Unlock()
25			return
26		}
27		type target struct {
28			ps *peerPathState
29			pr *telemetry.Prober
30		}
31		// Probe EVERY bound peer's paths (T93): a concentrator initiates its own probe stream to
32		// each edge over that edge-peer's per-(peer,path) prober, so every peer's liveness/RTT is
33		// measured for its OWN scheduler. On the single-peer edge/hub m.peers holds only the
34		// primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split single-peer sweep.
35		targets := make([]target, 0, len(m.paths))
36		for _, p := range m.peers {
37			for _, ps := range p.paths {
38				if ps.prober == nil {
39					continue
40				}
41				targets = append(targets, target{ps: ps, pr: ps.prober})
42			}
43		}
44		m.mu.Unlock()
45	
46		for _, t := range targets {
47			if remote, ok := t.ps.getRemote(); ok {
48				if raw, err := t.pr.SendProbe(); err == nil {
49					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe; this races no in-flight Send.
50					_, _ = t.ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(raw, remote)
51				}
52			}
53			t.pr.Tick()
54		}
55		// Eager failover nudge (defect D18): recompute the scheduler's active egress path
56		// each probe cadence so a liveness DOWN switches egress even when no application
57		// Send is driving Pick. This is the timer-driven companion to the receive-tick
58		// nudge (see tickLivenessFromReceive) — under CPU starvation this loop's ticker
59		// lags, which is why the receive-tick path carries the guarantee, but when the
60		// loop does run it keeps the selection fresh independent of egress traffic.
61		m.nudgeSchedulerActive()
62	}
63	
64	// StartProbeLoop launches the probe cadence goroutine: it calls emitProbes every
65	// interval until the returned stop function is invoked. The caller (device.Up)
66	// starts it AFTER the engine has opened the bind and stops it BEFORE Close, so the
67	// loop only runs while the sockets exist (emitProbes is a safe no-op if it races a
68	// closed bind). The returned stopper is idempotent.
69	//
70	// The cadence uses a wall-clock ticker because it is production timing glue, not
71	// liveness logic: every liveness decision the loop drives runs through the
72	// injected telemetry.Clock the Probers hold (SendProbe stamps it, Tick reads it),
73	// so tests drive emitProbes directly against a fake clock and never start this
74	// goroutine. It is a no-op (returning a no-op stopper) when the bind has no
75	// probers or interval <= 0.
76	func (m *Multipath) StartProbeLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
77		if m.probers == nil || interval <= 0 {
78			return func() {}
79		}
80		// Arm the receive-path liveness sweep at the same cadence (D15): a receiver may
81		// now advance liveness when the timer goroutine below is starved under load.
82		m.sweepIntervalNanos.Store(int64(interval))
83		done := make(chan struct{})
84		go func() {
85			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
86			defer ticker.Stop()
87			for {
88				select {
89				case <-done:
90					return
91				case <-ticker.C:
92					m.emitProbes()
93				}
94			}
95		}()
96		var once sync.Once
97		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
98	}
```

```go
1	package fec
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/binary"
5		"fmt"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// maxShards is the Reed-Solomon field limit: RS over GF(2^8) admits at most 256
10	// shards total (data + parity) per coding group.
11	const maxShards = 256
12	
13	// lenPrefixLen is the width of the big-endian length header prepended to each
14	// data payload before it is padded to the group's uniform shard length. It lets
15	// the decoder recover the exact original byte length after Reed-Solomon
16	// reconstruction, since RS operates only on fixed-length shards. A uint32 prefix
17	// covers any datagram this codec will ever wrap.
18	const lenPrefixLen = 4
19	
20	// ShardFramingOverhead is the fixed per-shard framing this codec adds on top of the
21	// caller's payload before Reed-Solomon coding — the big-endian length prefix. A
22	// parity shard's wire payload is ShardFramingOverhead + the group's largest coded
23	// data payload, so the multipath Bind uses it (with the outer parity/data header
24	// delta) to size the FEC MTU penalty (T24).
25	const ShardFramingOverhead = lenPrefixLen
26	
27	// Config parameterizes a Reed-Solomon FEC group: DataShards (N) opaque DATA
28	// frames are grouped and protected by ParityShards (K) parity frames, so the
29	// receiver recovers up to K arbitrary losses per group. Deadline bounds grouping
30	// latency: a group that does not fill to N within Deadline is flushed early over
31	// however many data frames it holds, so the parity is never delayed unboundedly
32	// waiting for a group to fill.
33	type Config struct {
34		// DataShards is N, the maximum number of DATA frames grouped before parity is
35		// emitted. Must be >= 1.
36		DataShards int
37		// ParityShards is K, the number of parity frames emitted per group and the
38		// maximum number of per-group losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1.
39		ParityShards int
40		// Deadline bounds the grouping latency: an open, partially-filled group is
41		// flushed (parity emitted over its current data frames) once this much time
42		// has elapsed since its first frame. Must be > 0.
43		Deadline time.Duration
44	}
45	
46	// Validate reports whether the configuration is a usable Reed-Solomon FEC ratio,
47	// failing fast on an out-of-range shard count or a non-positive deadline. It is the
48	// exported form of the same invariant NewEncoder/NewDecoder enforce, so a caller (the
49	// multipath Bind, T24) can reject a bad ratio before constructing either.
50	func (c Config) Validate() error { return c.validate() }
51	
52	func (c Config) validate() error {
53		if c.DataShards < 1 {
54			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
55		}
56		if c.ParityShards < 1 {
57			return fmt.Errorf("fec: ParityShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.ParityShards)
58		}
59		if c.DataShards+c.ParityShards > maxShards {
60			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards+ParityShards must be <= %d, got %d", maxShards, c.DataShards+c.ParityShards)
61		}
62		if c.Deadline <= 0 {
63			return fmt.Errorf("fec: Deadline must be > 0, got %s", c.Deadline)
64		}
65		return nil
66	}
67	
68	// GroupID identifies one FEC coding group. The encoder assigns group ids
69	// monotonically; the decoder buckets received shards by this id.
70	type GroupID uint32
71	
72	// Shard is the closed sum over the two kinds of frame an FEC group emits: a
73	// DataShard wrapping one opaque payload, and a ParityShard carrying redundancy.
74	// The unexported marker prevents other packages from introducing new kinds.
75	type Shard interface {
76		// GroupID returns the coding group this shard belongs to.
77		GroupID() GroupID
78		isShard()
79	}
80	
81	// DataShard is one opaque DATA payload admitted into a group, tagged with its
82	// coding coordinates so the decoder can place it. It carries no group cardinality
83	// (M), because a data frame is emitted immediately on admission — before the
84	// group's final size is known — to keep datapath latency low; the decoder learns
85	// M from any ParityShard of the group.
86	type DataShard struct {
87		Group   GroupID
88		Index   int    // position within the group, 0..M-1
89		Payload []byte // the opaque original bytes (never inspected)
90	}
91	
92	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
93	func (d DataShard) GroupID() GroupID { return d.Group }
94	func (DataShard) isShard()           {}
95	
96	// ParityShard is one Reed-Solomon parity frame for a group. It carries DataCount
97	// (M) and its Payload length equals the group's uniform shard length, so a single
98	// received parity frame tells the decoder both the group cardinality and the
99	// shard geometry it needs to reconstruct losses.
100	type ParityShard struct {
101		Group     GroupID
102		Index     int    // parity position, 0..K-1; RS shard index is DataCount+Index
103		DataCount int    // M: number of data shards this parity protects
104		Payload   []byte // parity bytes; len == group shard length
105	}
106	
107	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
108	func (p ParityShard) GroupID() GroupID { return p.Group }
109	func (ParityShard) isShard()           {}
110	
111	// Recovered is a data payload the decoder reconstructed from parity — one that
112	// was NOT directly received. Directly-received data frames are never echoed back
113	// as Recovered; the caller already holds them.
114	type Recovered struct {
115		Group   GroupID
116		Index   int
117		Payload []byte
118	}
119	
120	// encodeDataShard renders one payload as a fixed-length RS shard: a big-endian
121	// uint32 length prefix followed by the payload, right-padded with zeros to
122	// shardLen. shardLen is guaranteed >= lenPrefixLen+len(payload) by the caller.
123	func encodeDataShard(payload []byte, shardLen int) []byte {
124		s := make([]byte, shardLen)
125		binary.BigEndian.PutUint32(s, uint32(len(payload)))
126		copy(s[lenPrefixLen:], payload)
127		return s
128	}
129	
130	// decodeDataShard inverts encodeDataShard, returning the exact original payload.
131	// It fails fast on a length prefix that overruns the shard, which would indicate
132	// corruption or a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable condition.
133	func decodeDataShard(shard []byte) ([]byte, error) {
134		if len(shard) < lenPrefixLen {
135			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard too short for length prefix: %d bytes", len(shard))
136		}
137		n := int(binary.BigEndian.Uint32(shard[:lenPrefixLen]))
138		if n > len(shard)-lenPrefixLen {
139			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard length prefix %d overruns shard payload %d", n, len(shard)-lenPrefixLen)
140		}
141		out := make([]byte, n)
142		copy(out, shard[lenPrefixLen:lenPrefixLen+n])
143		return out, nil
144	}
```

```go
36	// New builds a JSON-handler Logger writing to w at the given level. An unknown
37	// level is a configuration error (fail fast); an empty level defaults to info.
38	func New(level string, w io.Writer) (Logger, error) {
39		lvl, err := parseLevel(level)
40		if err != nil {

42		}

52			return slog.LevelDebug, nil
53		case "warn", "warning":

55		case "error":

57		default:
```

**`internal/fec/fec.go`** — Config(struct)

```go
1	package fec
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/binary"
5		"fmt"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// maxShards is the Reed-Solomon field limit: RS over GF(2^8) admits at most 256
10	// shards total (data + parity) per coding group.
11	const maxShards = 256
12	
13	// lenPrefixLen is the width of the big-endian length header prepended to each
14	// data payload before it is padded to the group's uniform shard length. It lets
15	// the decoder recover the exact original byte length after Reed-Solomon
16	// reconstruction, since RS operates only on fixed-length shards. A uint32 prefix
17	// covers any datagram this codec will ever wrap.
18	const lenPrefixLen = 4
19	
20	// ShardFramingOverhead is the fixed per-shard framing this codec adds on top of the
21	// caller's payload before Reed-Solomon coding — the big-endian length prefix. A
22	// parity shard's wire payload is ShardFramingOverhead + the group's largest coded
23	// data payload, so the multipath Bind uses it (with the outer parity/data header
24	// delta) to size the FEC MTU penalty (T24).
25	const ShardFramingOverhead = lenPrefixLen
26	
27	// Config parameterizes a Reed-Solomon FEC group: DataShards (N) opaque DATA
28	// frames are grouped and protected by ParityShards (K) parity frames, so the
29	// receiver recovers up to K arbitrary losses per group. Deadline bounds grouping
30	// latency: a group that does not fill to N within Deadline is flushed early over
31	// however many data frames it holds, so the parity is never delayed unboundedly
32	// waiting for a group to fill.
33	type Config struct {
34		// DataShards is N, the maximum number of DATA frames grouped before parity is
35		// emitted. Must be >= 1.
36		DataShards int
37		// ParityShards is K, the number of parity frames emitted per group and the
38		// maximum number of per-group losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1.
39		ParityShards int
40		// Deadline bounds the grouping latency: an open, partially-filled group is
41		// flushed (parity emitted over its current data frames) once this much time
42		// has elapsed since its first frame. Must be > 0.
43		Deadline time.Duration
44	}
45	
46	// Validate reports whether the configuration is a usable Reed-Solomon FEC ratio,
47	// failing fast on an out-of-range shard count or a non-positive deadline. It is the
48	// exported form of the same invariant NewEncoder/NewDecoder enforce, so a caller (the
49	// multipath Bind, T24) can reject a bad ratio before constructing either.
50	func (c Config) Validate() error { return c.validate() }
51	
52	func (c Config) validate() error {
53		if c.DataShards < 1 {
54			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
55		}
56		if c.ParityShards < 1 {
57			return fmt.Errorf("fec: ParityShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.ParityShards)
58		}
59		if c.DataShards+c.ParityShards > maxShards {
60			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards+ParityShards must be <= %d, got %d", maxShards, c.DataShards+c.ParityShards)
61		}
62		if c.Deadline <= 0 {
63			return fmt.Errorf("fec: Deadline must be > 0, got %s", c.Deadline)
64		}
65		return nil
66	}
67	
68	// GroupID identifies one FEC coding group. The encoder assigns group ids
69	// monotonically; the decoder buckets received shards by this id.
70	type GroupID uint32
71	
72	// Shard is the closed sum over the two kinds of frame an FEC group emits: a
73	// DataShard wrapping one opaque payload, and a ParityShard carrying redundancy.
74	// The unexported marker prevents other packages from introducing new kinds.
75	type Shard interface {
76		// GroupID returns the coding group this shard belongs to.
77		GroupID() GroupID
78		isShard()
79	}
80	
81	// DataShard is one opaque DATA payload admitted into a group, tagged with its
82	// coding coordinates so the decoder can place it. It carries no group cardinality
83	// (M), because a data frame is emitted immediately on admission — before the
84	// group's final size is known — to keep datapath latency low; the decoder learns
85	// M from any ParityShard of the group.
86	type DataShard struct {
87		Group   GroupID
88		Index   int    // position within the group, 0..M-1
89		Payload []byte // the opaque original bytes (never inspected)
90	}
91	
92	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
93	func (d DataShard) GroupID() GroupID { return d.Group }
94	func (DataShard) isShard()           {}
95	
96	// ParityShard is one Reed-Solomon parity frame for a group. It carries DataCount
97	// (M) and its Payload length equals the group's uniform shard length, so a single
98	// received parity frame tells the decoder both the group cardinality and the
99	// shard geometry it needs to reconstruct losses.
100	type ParityShard struct {
101		Group     GroupID
102		Index     int    // parity position, 0..K-1; RS shard index is DataCount+Index
103		DataCount int    // M: number of data shards this parity protects
104		Payload   []byte // parity bytes; len == group shard length
105	}
106	
107	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
108	func (p ParityShard) GroupID() GroupID { return p.Group }
109	func (ParityShard) isShard()           {}
110	
111	// Recovered is a data payload the decoder reconstructed from parity — one that
112	// was NOT directly received. Directly-received data frames are never echoed back
113	// as Recovered; the caller already holds them.
114	type Recovered struct {
115		Group   GroupID
116		Index   int
117		Payload []byte
118	}
119	
120	// encodeDataShard renders one payload as a fixed-length RS shard: a big-endian
121	// uint32 length prefix followed by the payload, right-padded with zeros to
122	// shardLen. shardLen is guaranteed >= lenPrefixLen+len(payload) by the caller.
123	func encodeDataShard(payload []byte, shardLen int) []byte {
124		s := make([]byte, shardLen)
125		binary.BigEndian.PutUint32(s, uint32(len(payload)))
126		copy(s[lenPrefixLen:], payload)
127		return s
128	}
129	
130	// decodeDataShard inverts encodeDataShard, returning the exact original payload.
131	// It fails fast on a length prefix that overruns the shard, which would indicate
132	// corruption or a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable condition.
133	func decodeDataShard(shard []byte) ([]byte, error) {
134		if len(shard) < lenPrefixLen {
135			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard too short for length prefix: %d bytes", len(shard))
136		}
137		n := int(binary.BigEndian.Uint32(shard[:lenPrefixLen]))
138		if n > len(shard)-lenPrefixLen {
139			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard length prefix %d overruns shard payload %d", n, len(shard)-lenPrefixLen)
140		}
141		out := make([]byte, n)
142		copy(out, shard[lenPrefixLen:lenPrefixLen+n])
143		return out, nil
144	}
```

```go
86	func (e *Encoder) SetParity(parity int) {
87		if parity < 0 {
88			parity = 0
89		}
90		if parity > e.cfg.ParityShards {
91			parity = e.cfg.ParityShards
92		}
93		e.targetParity = parity

96	// Admit takes one opaque DATA payload into the current group and returns the
97	// DataShard the caller must transmit immediately. When this admission fills the
98	// group to N shards, the group is closed and its K ParityShards are returned;
99	// otherwise the parity slice is nil and the caller polls Tick to enforce the
100	// deadline. Admit copies payload, so the caller may reuse its buffer.
101	func (e *Encoder) Admit(payload []byte) (DataShard, []ParityShard, error) {
102		if !e.hasOpen {
103			e.openGroup()
104		}
105		idx := len(e.pending)
106		buf := make([]byte, len(payload))
107		copy(buf, payload)
108		e.pending = append(e.pending, buf)
109	
110		ds := DataShard{Group: e.group, Index: idx, Payload: append([]byte(nil), buf...)}
111	
112		if len(e.pending) >= e.cfg.DataShards {
113			parity, err := e.close()
114			if err != nil {
115				return DataShard{}, nil, err
116			}
117			return ds, parity, nil
118		}
119		return ds, nil, nil
120	}
121	
122	// Tick enforces the grouping deadline against the injected clock. If an open
123	// group has existed for at least Deadline, it is closed and its K ParityShards
124	// returned (coded over the M data shards admitted so far). Otherwise it returns
125	// nil. Tick is how a partially-filled — or entirely idle-then-single-frame —
126	// group still gets its parity within the deadline. Poll it at least as often as
127	// Deadline; NextDeadline reports when the next Tick is due.
128	func (e *Encoder) Tick() ([]ParityShard, error) {
129		if !e.hasOpen {
130			return nil, nil
131		}
132		if e.clock.Now().Sub(e.opened) < e.cfg.Deadline {
133			return nil, nil
134		}
135		return e.close()
136	}
137	
138	// Flush closes the current open group unconditionally (e.g. on shutdown),
139	// returning its K ParityShards, or nil if no group is open. It ignores the
140	// deadline.
141	func (e *Encoder) Flush() ([]ParityShard, error) {
142		if !e.hasOpen {
143			return nil, nil
144		}
145		return e.close()
146	}

148	// NextDeadline reports the instant at which the current open group must be
149	// flushed, and whether a group is currently open. A scheduler uses it to arm a

151	func (e *Encoder) NextDeadline() (time.Time, bool) {
152		if !e.hasOpen {
153			return time.Time{}, false
154		}
155		return e.opened.Add(e.cfg.Deadline), true
156	}

158	func (e *Encoder) openGroup() {
159		e.hasOpen = true
160		e.group = e.nextGroup
161		e.nextGroup++
162		e.opened = e.clock.Now()
163		e.groupParity = e.targetParity
164		e.pending = e.pending[:0]
165	}

167	// close codes the open group's M pending data shards into K parity shards and
168	// resets open-group state. It never returns nil parity for a non-empty group.
169	func (e *Encoder) close() ([]ParityShard, error) {
170		m := len(e.pending)
171		if m == 0 {
172			// Unreachable: a group only opens on Admit, so it always holds >= 1 shard.
173			e.hasOpen = false
174			return nil, nil
175		}
176	
177		// A group opened with a zero parity target carries no redundancy: its data frames
178		// were already emitted on admission, so close it without coding any parity. The
179		// receiver never learns this group's cardinality (no parity frame) and simply
180		// delivers whatever data survives — which is the intended "no FEC this group"
181		// semantics the adaptive controller selects when loss is low (T29).
182		k := e.groupParity
183		if k == 0 {
184			e.hasOpen = false
185			return nil, nil
186		}
187	
188		shardLen := lenPrefixLen
189		for _, p := range e.pending {
190			if l := lenPrefixLen + len(p); l > shardLen {
191				shardLen = l
192			}
193		}
194	
195		codec, err := e.codec(m, k)
196		if err != nil {
197			return nil, err
198		}
199	
200		shards := make([][]byte, m+k)
201		for i, p := range e.pending {
202			shards[i] = encodeDataShard(p, shardLen)
203		}
204		for j := 0; j < k; j++ {
205			shards[m+j] = make([]byte, shardLen)
206		}
207		if err := codec.Encode(shards); err != nil {
208			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: reed-solomon encode (m=%d k=%d len=%d): %w", m, k, shardLen, err)
209		}
210	
211		group := e.group
212		parity := make([]ParityShard, k)
213		for j := 0; j < k; j++ {
214			parity[j] = ParityShard{
215				Group:     group,
216				Index:     j,
217				DataCount: m,
218				Payload:   shards[m+j],
219			}
220		}
221	
222		e.hasOpen = false
223		return parity, nil
224	}
```

```go
83	// controller (internal/adaptivefec, T29) drives this; fixed-ratio callers never
84	// touch it. Like the rest of the Encoder it is NOT safe for concurrent use: the
85	// datapath serializes SetParity with Admit/Tick/Flush under its own send lock.
86	func (e *Encoder) SetParity(parity int) {
87		if parity < 0 {
88			parity = 0
89		}
90		if parity > e.cfg.ParityShards {
91			parity = e.cfg.ParityShards
92		}
93		e.targetParity = parity
94	}
95	
96	// Admit takes one opaque DATA payload into the current group and returns the
97	// DataShard the caller must transmit immediately. When this admission fills the
98	// group to N shards, the group is closed and its K ParityShards are returned;
99	// otherwise the parity slice is nil and the caller polls Tick to enforce the
100	// deadline. Admit copies payload, so the caller may reuse its buffer.
101	func (e *Encoder) Admit(payload []byte) (DataShard, []ParityShard, error) {
102		if !e.hasOpen {
103			e.openGroup()
104		}
105		idx := len(e.pending)
106		buf := make([]byte, len(payload))
107		copy(buf, payload)
108		e.pending = append(e.pending, buf)
109	
110		ds := DataShard{Group: e.group, Index: idx, Payload: append([]byte(nil), buf...)}
111	
112		if len(e.pending) >= e.cfg.DataShards {
113			parity, err := e.close()
114			if err != nil {
115				return DataShard{}, nil, err
116			}
117			return ds, parity, nil
118		}
119		return ds, nil, nil
120	}
121	
122	// Tick enforces the grouping deadline against the injected clock. If an open
123	// group has existed for at least Deadline, it is closed and its K ParityShards
124	// returned (coded over the M data shards admitted so far). Otherwise it returns
125	// nil. Tick is how a partially-filled — or entirely idle-then-single-frame —
126	// group still gets its parity within the deadline. Poll it at least as often as
127	// Deadline; NextDeadline reports when the next Tick is due.
128	func (e *Encoder) Tick() ([]ParityShard, error) {
129		if !e.hasOpen {
130			return nil, nil
131		}
132		if e.clock.Now().Sub(e.opened) < e.cfg.Deadline {
133			return nil, nil
134		}
135		return e.close()
136	}
137	
138	// Flush closes the current open group unconditionally (e.g. on shutdown),
139	// returning its K ParityShards, or nil if no group is open. It ignores the
140	// deadline.
141	func (e *Encoder) Flush() ([]ParityShard, error) {
142		if !e.hasOpen {
143			return nil, nil
144		}
145		return e.close()
146	}
147	
148	// NextDeadline reports the instant at which the current open group must be
149	// flushed, and whether a group is currently open. A scheduler uses it to arm a
150	// timer instead of busy-polling Tick.
151	func (e *Encoder) NextDeadline() (time.Time, bool) {
152		if !e.hasOpen {
153			return time.Time{}, false
154		}
155		return e.opened.Add(e.cfg.Deadline), true
156	}
157	
158	func (e *Encoder) openGroup() {
159		e.hasOpen = true
160		e.group = e.nextGroup
161		e.nextGroup++
162		e.opened = e.clock.Now()
163		e.groupParity = e.targetParity
164		e.pending = e.pending[:0]
165	}
166	
167	// close codes the open group's M pending data shards into K parity shards and
168	// resets open-group state. It never returns nil parity for a non-empty group.
169	func (e *Encoder) close() ([]ParityShard, error) {
170		m := len(e.pending)
171		if m == 0 {
172			// Unreachable: a group only opens on Admit, so it always holds >= 1 shard.
173			e.hasOpen = false
174			return nil, nil
175		}
176	
177		// A group opened with a zero parity target carries no redundancy: its data frames
178		// were already emitted on admission, so close it without coding any parity. The
179		// receiver never learns this group's cardinality (no parity frame) and simply
180		// delivers whatever data survives — which is the intended "no FEC this group"
181		// semantics the adaptive controller selects when loss is low (T29).
182		k := e.groupParity
183		if k == 0 {
184			e.hasOpen = false
185			return nil, nil
186		}
187	
188		shardLen := lenPrefixLen
189		for _, p := range e.pending {
190			if l := lenPrefixLen + len(p); l > shardLen {
191				shardLen = l
192			}
193		}
194	
195		codec, err := e.codec(m, k)
196		if err != nil {
197			return nil, err
198		}
199	
200		shards := make([][]byte, m+k)
201		for i, p := range e.pending {
202			shards[i] = encodeDataShard(p, shardLen)
203		}
204		for j := 0; j < k; j++ {
205			shards[m+j] = make([]byte, shardLen)
206		}
207		if err := codec.Encode(shards); err != nil {
208			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: reed-solomon encode (m=%d k=%d len=%d): %w", m, k, shardLen, err)
209		}
210	
211		group := e.group
212		parity := make([]ParityShard, k)
213		for j := 0; j < k; j++ {
214			parity[j] = ParityShard{
215				Group:     group,
216				Index:     j,
217				DataCount: m,
218				Payload:   shards[m+j],
219			}
220		}
221	
222		e.hasOpen = false
223		return parity, nil
224	}
225

227	// shards, building it once on first use. In fixed mode k is always cfg.ParityShards;
```

```go
3	import (

10	
11	// trafficProvider is the read seam the metrics adapter consumes: the multipath Bind's
12	// per-PEER traffic+telemetry, FEC, and resequencer snapshot (T93/T94). *bind.Multipath
13	// satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in the adapter's unit test, so the mapping and rate
14	// derivation are testable without a running engine. PeerSnapshots generalizes the
15	// pre-T94 PathSnapshots/FECSnapshot (primary-peer-only) to every bound peer, so a
16	// single-peer edge/hub still maps 1:1 onto today's series (len(PeerSnapshots())==1) and
17	// a multi-peer concentrator's additional peers surface as additional entries.
18	type trafficProvider interface {
19		PeerSnapshots() []bind.PeerSnapshot
20	}
21	
22	// sampleKey identifies one (peer,path) pair in the throughput last-sample map (T94): a
23	// path name alone is only unique WITHIN one peer, not across peers (each peer's path
24	// set is independently configured/named in principle), so keying by path name alone
25	// would let two peers' samples for a same-named path clobber each other's rate
26	// derivation. peer is "" for the single-peer edge/hub and a concentrator's primary,
27	// exactly matching PeerSnapshot.Name — so a single-peer config's map degenerates to the
28	// pre-T94 keying (peer is always "", so the key varies only by path).
29	type sampleKey struct {
30		peer, path string
31	}
32	
33	// metricsSource adapts the multipath Bind onto metrics.Source: at every scrape it
34	// reads the Bind's cumulative per-(peer,path) byte counters + telemetry, and DERIVES
35	// the per-(peer,path) throughput as the rate of the (tx+rx) byte-counter delta since
36	// the previous scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the
37	// Bind's hot path a pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the
38	// only stateful, scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a
39	// (peer,path) reports zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move
40	// backwards (only possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that
41	// interval rather than a spurious negative.
42	type metricsSource struct {
43		provider trafficProvider
44		// session yields the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2), read from the
45		// engine at scrape time. It is separate from provider because the bind stays
46		// WG-unaware — the session signal is sourced by the device layer's engine seam.
47		session sessionSnapshotter
48		clock   telemetry.Clock
49	
50		mu   sync.Mutex
51		last map[sampleKey]byteSample
52	}
53	
54	// byteSample is the previous scrape's cumulative (tx+rx) total for one (peer,path) and
55	// the instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.
56	type byteSample struct {
57		total   uint64
58		atNanos int64
59	}
60	
61	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind (per-peer path traffic, FEC,
62	// and resequencer counters) and the engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The
63	// clock is injected so the throughput derivation is deterministic under test.
64	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
65		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[sampleKey]byteSample)}
66	}
67	
68	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
69	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-
70	// (peer,path) last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PeerSnapshots() reads
71	// atomics and each prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock
72	// across a prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
73	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
75		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
76	
77		total := 0
78		for _, peer := range peers {
79			total += len(peer.Paths)
80		}
81	
82		s.mu.Lock()
83		defer s.mu.Unlock()
84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)
85		for _, peer := range peers {
86			for _, t := range peer.Paths {
87				key := sampleKey{peer: peer.Name, path: t.Name}
88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
89				var tput float64
90				if prev, ok := s.last[key]; ok {
91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}
110	
111	// FEC implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer connection-scoped FEC
112	// counters (T24, T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer. Unlike the
113	// per-path throughput, the FEC counters are cumulative and need no rate derivation or
114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
115	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() []metrics.FECSnapshot {
116		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
117		out := make([]metrics.FECSnapshot, len(peers))
118		for i, peer := range peers {
119			f := peer.FEC
120			out[i] = metrics.FECSnapshot{
121				Peer:                 peer.Name,
122				DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,
123				RepairPackets:        f.ParityFrames,
124				RecoveredPackets:     f.Recovered,
125				UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
126				DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
127				RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
128				ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
129			}
130		}
131		return out
132	}
133	
134	// Reseq implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer resequencer counters
135	// (T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer — a direct pass-through of
136	// the resequencer's own cumulative Stats(), like FEC needing no rate derivation.
137	func (s *metricsSource) Reseq() []metrics.ReseqSnapshot {
138		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
139		out := make([]metrics.ReseqSnapshot, len(peers))
140		for i, peer := range peers {
141			out[i] = metrics.ReseqSnapshot{Peer: peer.Name, Stats: peer.Reseq}
142		}
143		return out
144	}
145	
146	// PeerNames implements metrics.Source: it returns the current bound-peer name set,
147	// queried once by metrics.NewCollector to fix the `peer` label's presence for the
148	// collector's whole life (T94) — see the metrics package doc for the back-compat rule.
149	func (s *metricsSource) PeerNames() []string {
150		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
151		names := make([]string, len(peers))
152		for i, peer := range peers {
153			names[i] = peer.Name
154		}
155		return names
156	}
157	
158	// Session implements metrics.Source: it reads the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot
159	// (I2) from the engine seam at scrape time. Like FEC it is a direct pass-through of a
160	// snapshot the underlying monitor computes on demand — no per-scrape state is kept here
161	// (the 0->1 edge log is driven by the separate session-monitor poll loop, not by scrapes).
162	func (s *metricsSource) Session() metrics.SessionSnapshot {
163		return s.session.SessionSnapshot()
164	}
```

**`internal/fec/fec.go`** — Config(struct), Validate(method), validate(method)

```go
1	package fec
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/binary"
5		"fmt"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// maxShards is the Reed-Solomon field limit: RS over GF(2^8) admits at most 256
10	// shards total (data + parity) per coding group.
11	const maxShards = 256
12	
13	// lenPrefixLen is the width of the big-endian length header prepended to each
14	// data payload before it is padded to the group's uniform shard length. It lets
15	// the decoder recover the exact original byte length after Reed-Solomon
16	// reconstruction, since RS operates only on fixed-length shards. A uint32 prefix
17	// covers any datagram this codec will ever wrap.
18	const lenPrefixLen = 4
19	
20	// ShardFramingOverhead is the fixed per-shard framing this codec adds on top of the
21	// caller's payload before Reed-Solomon coding — the big-endian length prefix. A
22	// parity shard's wire payload is ShardFramingOverhead + the group's largest coded
23	// data payload, so the multipath Bind uses it (with the outer parity/data header
24	// delta) to size the FEC MTU penalty (T24).
25	const ShardFramingOverhead = lenPrefixLen
26	
27	// Config parameterizes a Reed-Solomon FEC group: DataShards (N) opaque DATA
28	// frames are grouped and protected by ParityShards (K) parity frames, so the
29	// receiver recovers up to K arbitrary losses per group. Deadline bounds grouping
30	// latency: a group that does not fill to N within Deadline is flushed early over
31	// however many data frames it holds, so the parity is never delayed unboundedly
32	// waiting for a group to fill.
33	type Config struct {
34		// DataShards is N, the maximum number of DATA frames grouped before parity is
35		// emitted. Must be >= 1.
36		DataShards int
37		// ParityShards is K, the number of parity frames emitted per group and the
38		// maximum number of per-group losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1.
39		ParityShards int
40		// Deadline bounds the grouping latency: an open, partially-filled group is
41		// flushed (parity emitted over its current data frames) once this much time
42		// has elapsed since its first frame. Must be > 0.
43		Deadline time.Duration
44	}
45	
46	// Validate reports whether the configuration is a usable Reed-Solomon FEC ratio,
47	// failing fast on an out-of-range shard count or a non-positive deadline. It is the
48	// exported form of the same invariant NewEncoder/NewDecoder enforce, so a caller (the
49	// multipath Bind, T24) can reject a bad ratio before constructing either.
50	func (c Config) Validate() error { return c.validate() }
51	
52	func (c Config) validate() error {
53		if c.DataShards < 1 {
54			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
55		}
56		if c.ParityShards < 1 {
57			return fmt.Errorf("fec: ParityShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.ParityShards)
58		}
59		if c.DataShards+c.ParityShards > maxShards {
60			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards+ParityShards must be <= %d, got %d", maxShards, c.DataShards+c.ParityShards)
61		}
62		if c.Deadline <= 0 {
63			return fmt.Errorf("fec: Deadline must be > 0, got %s", c.Deadline)
64		}
65		return nil
66	}
67	
68	// GroupID identifies one FEC coding group. The encoder assigns group ids
69	// monotonically; the decoder buckets received shards by this id.
70	type GroupID uint32
71	
72	// Shard is the closed sum over the two kinds of frame an FEC group emits: a
73	// DataShard wrapping one opaque payload, and a ParityShard carrying redundancy.
74	// The unexported marker prevents other packages from introducing new kinds.
75	type Shard interface {
76		// GroupID returns the coding group this shard belongs to.
77		GroupID() GroupID
78		isShard()
79	}
80	
81	// DataShard is one opaque DATA payload admitted into a group, tagged with its
82	// coding coordinates so the decoder can place it. It carries no group cardinality
83	// (M), because a data frame is emitted immediately on admission — before the
84	// group's final size is known — to keep datapath latency low; the decoder learns
85	// M from any ParityShard of the group.
86	type DataShard struct {
87		Group   GroupID
88		Index   int    // position within the group, 0..M-1
89		Payload []byte // the opaque original bytes (never inspected)
90	}
91	
92	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
93	func (d DataShard) GroupID() GroupID { return d.Group }
94	func (DataShard) isShard()           {}
95	
96	// ParityShard is one Reed-Solomon parity frame for a group. It carries DataCount
97	// (M) and its Payload length equals the group's uniform shard length, so a single
98	// received parity frame tells the decoder both the group cardinality and the
99	// shard geometry it needs to reconstruct losses.
100	type ParityShard struct {
101		Group     GroupID
102		Index     int    // parity position, 0..K-1; RS shard index is DataCount+Index
103		DataCount int    // M: number of data shards this parity protects
104		Payload   []byte // parity bytes; len == group shard length
105	}
106	
107	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
108	func (p ParityShard) GroupID() GroupID { return p.Group }
109	func (ParityShard) isShard()           {}
110	
111	// Recovered is a data payload the decoder reconstructed from parity — one that
112	// was NOT directly received. Directly-received data frames are never echoed back
113	// as Recovered; the caller already holds them.
114	type Recovered struct {
115		Group   GroupID
116		Index   int
117		Payload []byte
118	}
119	
120	// encodeDataShard renders one payload as a fixed-length RS shard: a big-endian
121	// uint32 length prefix followed by the payload, right-padded with zeros to
122	// shardLen. shardLen is guaranteed >= lenPrefixLen+len(payload) by the caller.
123	func encodeDataShard(payload []byte, shardLen int) []byte {
124		s := make([]byte, shardLen)
125		binary.BigEndian.PutUint32(s, uint32(len(payload)))
126		copy(s[lenPrefixLen:], payload)
127		return s
128	}
129	
130	// decodeDataShard inverts encodeDataShard, returning the exact original payload.
131	// It fails fast on a length prefix that overruns the shard, which would indicate
132	// corruption or a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable condition.
133	func decodeDataShard(shard []byte) ([]byte, error) {
134		if len(shard) < lenPrefixLen {
135			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard too short for length prefix: %d bytes", len(shard))
136		}
137		n := int(binary.BigEndian.Uint32(shard[:lenPrefixLen]))
138		if n > len(shard)-lenPrefixLen {
139			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard length prefix %d overruns shard payload %d", n, len(shard)-lenPrefixLen)
140		}
141		out := make([]byte, n)
142		copy(out, shard[lenPrefixLen:lenPrefixLen+n])
143		return out, nil
144	}
```
