```go
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"sync"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
7		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
9	)
10	
11	// trafficProvider is the read seam the metrics adapter consumes: the multipath Bind's
12	// per-PEER traffic+telemetry, FEC, and resequencer snapshot (T93/T94). *bind.Multipath
13	// satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in the adapter's unit test, so the mapping and rate
14	// derivation are testable without a running engine. PeerSnapshots generalizes the
15	// pre-T94 PathSnapshots/FECSnapshot (primary-peer-only) to every bound peer, so a
16	// single-peer edge/hub still maps 1:1 onto today's series (len(PeerSnapshots())==1) and
17	// a multi-peer concentrator's additional peers surface as additional entries.
18	type trafficProvider interface {
19		PeerSnapshots() []bind.PeerSnapshot
20	}
21	
22	// sampleKey identifies one (peer,path) pair in the throughput last-sample map (T94): a
23	// path name alone is only unique WITHIN one peer, not across peers (each peer's path
24	// set is independently configured/named in principle), so keying by path name alone
25	// would let two peers' samples for a same-named path clobber each other's rate
26	// derivation. peer is "" for the single-peer edge/hub and a concentrator's primary,
27	// exactly matching PeerSnapshot.Name — so a single-peer config's map degenerates to the
28	// pre-T94 keying (peer is always "", so the key varies only by path).
29	type sampleKey struct {
30		peer, path string
31	}
32	
33	// metricsSource adapts the multipath Bind onto metrics.Source: at every scrape it
34	// reads the Bind's cumulative per-(peer,path) byte counters + telemetry, and DERIVES
35	// the per-(peer,path) throughput as the rate of the (tx+rx) byte-counter delta since
36	// the previous scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the
37	// Bind's hot path a pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the
38	// only stateful, scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a
39	// (peer,path) reports zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move
40	// backwards (only possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that
41	// interval rather than a spurious negative.
42	type metricsSource struct {
43		provider trafficProvider
44		// session yields the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2), read from the
45		// engine at scrape time. It is separate from provider because the bind stays
46		// WG-unaware — the session signal is sourced by the device layer's engine seam.
47		session sessionSnapshotter
48		clock   telemetry.Clock
49	
50		mu   sync.Mutex
51		last map[sampleKey]byteSample
52	}
53	
54	// byteSample is the previous scrape's cumulative (tx+rx) total for one (peer,path) and
55	// the instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.
56	type byteSample struct {
57		total   uint64
58		atNanos int64
59	}
60	
61	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind (per-peer path traffic, FEC,
62	// and resequencer counters) and the engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The
63	// clock is injected so the throughput derivation is deterministic under test.
64	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
65		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[sampleKey]byteSample)}
66	}
67	
68	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
69	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-
70	// (peer,path) last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PeerSnapshots() reads
71	// atomics and each prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock
72	// across a prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
73	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
75		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
76	
77		total := 0
78		for _, peer := range peers {
79			total += len(peer.Paths)
80		}
81	
82		s.mu.Lock()
83		defer s.mu.Unlock()
84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)
85		for _, peer := range peers {
86			for _, t := range peer.Paths {
87				key := sampleKey{peer: peer.Name, path: t.Name}
88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
89				var tput float64
90				if prev, ok := s.last[key]; ok {
91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}
110	
111	// FEC implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer connection-scoped FEC
112	// counters (T24, T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer. Unlike the
113	// per-path throughput, the FEC counters are cumulative and need no rate derivation or
114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
115	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() []metrics.FECSnapshot {
116		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
117		out := make([]metrics.FECSnapshot, len(peers))
118		for i, peer := range peers {
119			f := peer.FEC
120			out[i] = metrics.FECSnapshot{
121				Peer:                 peer.Name,
122				DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,
123				RepairPackets:        f.ParityFrames,
124				RecoveredPackets:     f.Recovered,
125				UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
126				DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
127				RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
128				ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
129			}
130		}
131		return out
132	}
133	
134	// Reseq implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer resequencer counters
135	// (T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer — a direct pass-through of
136	// the resequencer's own cumulative Stats(), like FEC needing no rate derivation.
137	func (s *metricsSource) Reseq() []metrics.ReseqSnapshot {
138		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
139		out := make([]metrics.ReseqSnapshot, len(peers))
140		for i, peer := range peers {
141			out[i] = metrics.ReseqSnapshot{Peer: peer.Name, Stats: peer.Reseq}
142		}
143		return out
144	}
145	
146	// PeerNames implements metrics.Source: it returns the current bound-peer name set,
147	// queried once by metrics.NewCollector to fix the `peer` label's presence for the
148	// collector's whole life (T94) — see the metrics package doc for the back-compat rule.
149	func (s *metricsSource) PeerNames() []string {
150		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
151		names := make([]string, len(peers))
152		for i, peer := range peers {
153			names[i] = peer.Name
154		}
155		return names
156	}
157	
158	// Session implements metrics.Source: it reads the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot
159	// (I2) from the engine seam at scrape time. Like FEC it is a direct pass-through of a
160	// snapshot the underlying monitor computes on demand — no per-scrape state is kept here
161	// (the 0->1 edge log is driven by the separate session-monitor poll loop, not by scrapes).
162	func (s *metricsSource) Session() metrics.SessionSnapshot {
163		return s.session.SessionSnapshot()
164	}
```

```go
1	package fec
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/binary"
5		"fmt"
6		"time"
7	)
8	
9	// maxShards is the Reed-Solomon field limit: RS over GF(2^8) admits at most 256
10	// shards total (data + parity) per coding group.
11	const maxShards = 256
12	
13	// lenPrefixLen is the width of the big-endian length header prepended to each
14	// data payload before it is padded to the group's uniform shard length. It lets
15	// the decoder recover the exact original byte length after Reed-Solomon
16	// reconstruction, since RS operates only on fixed-length shards. A uint32 prefix
17	// covers any datagram this codec will ever wrap.
18	const lenPrefixLen = 4
19	
20	// ShardFramingOverhead is the fixed per-shard framing this codec adds on top of the
21	// caller's payload before Reed-Solomon coding — the big-endian length prefix. A
22	// parity shard's wire payload is ShardFramingOverhead + the group's largest coded
23	// data payload, so the multipath Bind uses it (with the outer parity/data header
24	// delta) to size the FEC MTU penalty (T24).
25	const ShardFramingOverhead = lenPrefixLen
26	
27	// Config parameterizes a Reed-Solomon FEC group: DataShards (N) opaque DATA
28	// frames are grouped and protected by ParityShards (K) parity frames, so the
29	// receiver recovers up to K arbitrary losses per group. Deadline bounds grouping
30	// latency: a group that does not fill to N within Deadline is flushed early over
31	// however many data frames it holds, so the parity is never delayed unboundedly
32	// waiting for a group to fill.
33	type Config struct {
34		// DataShards is N, the maximum number of DATA frames grouped before parity is
35		// emitted. Must be >= 1.
36		DataShards int
37		// ParityShards is K, the number of parity frames emitted per group and the
38		// maximum number of per-group losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1.
39		ParityShards int
40		// Deadline bounds the grouping latency: an open, partially-filled group is
41		// flushed (parity emitted over its current data frames) once this much time
42		// has elapsed since its first frame. Must be > 0.
43		Deadline time.Duration
44	}
45	
46	// Validate reports whether the configuration is a usable Reed-Solomon FEC ratio,
47	// failing fast on an out-of-range shard count or a non-positive deadline. It is the
48	// exported form of the same invariant NewEncoder/NewDecoder enforce, so a caller (the
49	// multipath Bind, T24) can reject a bad ratio before constructing either.
50	func (c Config) Validate() error { return c.validate() }
51	
52	func (c Config) validate() error {
53		if c.DataShards < 1 {
54			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.DataShards)
55		}
56		if c.ParityShards < 1 {
57			return fmt.Errorf("fec: ParityShards must be >= 1, got %d", c.ParityShards)
58		}
59		if c.DataShards+c.ParityShards > maxShards {
60			return fmt.Errorf("fec: DataShards+ParityShards must be <= %d, got %d", maxShards, c.DataShards+c.ParityShards)
61		}
62		if c.Deadline <= 0 {
63			return fmt.Errorf("fec: Deadline must be > 0, got %s", c.Deadline)
64		}
65		return nil
66	}
67	
68	// GroupID identifies one FEC coding group. The encoder assigns group ids
69	// monotonically; the decoder buckets received shards by this id.
70	type GroupID uint32
71	
72	// Shard is the closed sum over the two kinds of frame an FEC group emits: a
73	// DataShard wrapping one opaque payload, and a ParityShard carrying redundancy.
74	// The unexported marker prevents other packages from introducing new kinds.
75	type Shard interface {
76		// GroupID returns the coding group this shard belongs to.
77		GroupID() GroupID
78		isShard()
79	}
80	
81	// DataShard is one opaque DATA payload admitted into a group, tagged with its
82	// coding coordinates so the decoder can place it. It carries no group cardinality
83	// (M), because a data frame is emitted immediately on admission — before the
84	// group's final size is known — to keep datapath latency low; the decoder learns
85	// M from any ParityShard of the group.
86	type DataShard struct {
87		Group   GroupID
88		Index   int    // position within the group, 0..M-1
89		Payload []byte // the opaque original bytes (never inspected)
90	}
91	
92	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
93	func (d DataShard) GroupID() GroupID { return d.Group }
94	func (DataShard) isShard()           {}
95	
96	// ParityShard is one Reed-Solomon parity frame for a group. It carries DataCount
97	// (M) and its Payload length equals the group's uniform shard length, so a single
98	// received parity frame tells the decoder both the group cardinality and the
99	// shard geometry it needs to reconstruct losses.
100	type ParityShard struct {
101		Group     GroupID
102		Index     int    // parity position, 0..K-1; RS shard index is DataCount+Index
103		DataCount int    // M: number of data shards this parity protects
104		Payload   []byte // parity bytes; len == group shard length
105	}
106	
107	// GroupID returns the shard's coding group.
108	func (p ParityShard) GroupID() GroupID { return p.Group }
109	func (ParityShard) isShard()           {}
110	
111	// Recovered is a data payload the decoder reconstructed from parity — one that
112	// was NOT directly received. Directly-received data frames are never echoed back
113	// as Recovered; the caller already holds them.
114	type Recovered struct {
115		Group   GroupID
116		Index   int
117		Payload []byte
118	}
119	
120	// encodeDataShard renders one payload as a fixed-length RS shard: a big-endian
121	// uint32 length prefix followed by the payload, right-padded with zeros to
122	// shardLen. shardLen is guaranteed >= lenPrefixLen+len(payload) by the caller.
123	func encodeDataShard(payload []byte, shardLen int) []byte {
124		s := make([]byte, shardLen)
125		binary.BigEndian.PutUint32(s, uint32(len(payload)))
126		copy(s[lenPrefixLen:], payload)
127		return s
128	}
129	
130	// decodeDataShard inverts encodeDataShard, returning the exact original payload.
131	// It fails fast on a length prefix that overruns the shard, which would indicate
132	// corruption or a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable condition.
133	func decodeDataShard(shard []byte) ([]byte, error) {
134		if len(shard) < lenPrefixLen {
135			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard too short for length prefix: %d bytes", len(shard))
136		}
137		n := int(binary.BigEndian.Uint32(shard[:lenPrefixLen]))
138		if n > len(shard)-lenPrefixLen {
139			return nil, fmt.Errorf("fec: shard length prefix %d overruns shard payload %d", n, len(shard)-lenPrefixLen)
140		}
141		out := make([]byte, n)
142		copy(out, shard[lenPrefixLen:lenPrefixLen+n])
143		return out, nil
144	}
```

```go
1	package telemetry
2	
3	import (
4		"time"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injectable time source the liveness machinery reads. Production
10	// code passes SystemClock; tests pass a hand-advanced fake so transitions are
11	// deterministic and instant.
12	type Clock interface {
13		Now() time.Time
14	}
15	
16	// SystemClock is the wall-clock Clock.
17	type SystemClock struct{}
18	
19	// Now returns the current wall-clock time.
20	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
21	
22	// PathState is the liveness verdict for one path. Path liveness is entirely this
23	// codec's concern: the inner WireGuard keepalive is per-peer, not per-path, so a
24	// dead uplink under a live peer is invisible to WireGuard and must be detected
25	// here.
26	type PathState uint8
27	
28	const (
29		// StateDown means no fresh probe echo has been observed within the detection
30		// threshold; the path is presumed unusable.
31		StateDown PathState = iota
32		// StateUp means the path has recently returned probe echoes.
33		StateUp
34	)
35	
36	// String renders the state as the lowercase token used in structured logs.
37	func (s PathState) String() string {
38		switch s {
39		case StateUp:
40			return "up"
41		case StateDown:
42			return "down"
43		default:
44			return "unknown"
45		}
46	}
47	
48	// LivenessConfig holds the up/down detection thresholds. DownAfter is the
49	// silence duration that marks an up path down; UpAfterSuccesses is the number of
50	// consecutive echoes required to bring a down path up. The asymmetry is the
51	// hysteresis: a single lost echo never flaps the path, and a single recovered
52	// echo never prematurely declares it healthy.
53	type LivenessConfig struct {
54		DownAfter        time.Duration
55		UpAfterSuccesses int
56	}
57	
58	// Default per-path liveness detection timing (T13/T37/T39). These are the SINGLE
59	// SOURCE OF TRUTH for probe cadence and up/down hysteresis. The daemon
60	// (internal/device) builds its ProberConfig from them and runs its probe loop at
61	// DefaultProbeInterval; the e2e acceptance table (test/e2e/thresholds.go) derives
62	// its PLiveness* budget from them. Neither can silently diverge from the timing the
63	// daemon actually runs, which is the D16 reconciliation: one place to retune.
64	//
65	//   - DefaultDownAfter is the silence that marks an UP path DOWN.
66	//   - DefaultProbeInterval is the probe emission AND liveness Tick cadence; detection
67	//     latency is therefore bounded by DownAfter + one interval.
68	//   - DefaultUpSuccesses is the consecutive echoes that bring a DOWN path UP.
69	//   - DefaultLossWindow (0) takes the estimator's default trailing window.
70	//
71	// DownAfter spans DefaultProbeInterval*6, so SIX consecutive lost echoes are needed
72	// before a false DOWN — the D13 jitter-safety margin. T39 tightened the pair from
73	// 1500ms/250ms to 1200ms/200ms to WIDEN the P1 3s-recovery margin while KEEPING
74	// that six-lost-echo false-down tolerance exactly (the ratio is unchanged). Detection
75	// latency drops from ~1.75s to ~1.4s worst case, and the receive-path liveness sweep
76	// (bind, D15) makes that detection robust to probe-loop-ticker starvation under load.
77	const (
78		DefaultProbeInterval = 200 * time.Millisecond
79		DefaultDownAfter     = 1200 * time.Millisecond
80		DefaultUpSuccesses   = 3
81		DefaultLossWindow    = 0
82	)
83	
84	// Liveness is the per-path up/down state machine. It is driven by two events:
85	// RecordEcho on every authenticated probe echo, and Tick on a periodic timer.
86	// It starts Down and requires UpAfterSuccesses echoes before declaring Up, so a
87	// path is never reported healthy before it has actually answered.
88	type Liveness struct {
89		cfg   LivenessConfig
90		clock Clock
91		log   log.Logger
92	
93		state      PathState
94		lastGood   time.Time
95		haveGood   bool
96		goodStreak int
97	}
98	
99	// NewLiveness builds a per-path liveness machine. The supplied logger is tagged
100	// with the path name so every transition record carries the per-path field.
101	func NewLiveness(pathName string, cfg LivenessConfig, clock Clock, logger log.Logger) *Liveness {
102		return &Liveness{
103			cfg:   cfg,
104			clock: clock,
105			log:   logger.Path(pathName),
106			state: StateDown,
107		}
108	}
109	
110	// RecordEcho registers a successful, authenticated probe echo as a liveness
111	// heartbeat. Only echoes that are CONSECUTIVE — no more than DownAfter apart —
112	// accumulate toward Up; an echo that arrives after a longer silence starts the
113	// streak over, so UpAfterSuccesses stray echoes spread across arbitrary silence
114	// never bring a Down path Up. Once UpAfterSuccesses consecutive heartbeats
115	// accumulate, a Down path transitions Up.
116	func (l *Liveness) RecordEcho() {
117		now := l.clock.Now()
118		if l.haveGood && now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter {
119			l.goodStreak = 0 // silence broke the run of consecutive echoes
120		}
121		l.lastGood = now
122		l.haveGood = true
123		l.goodStreak++
124		if l.state == StateDown && l.goodStreak >= l.cfg.UpAfterSuccesses {
125			l.transition(StateUp, now)
126		}
127	}
128	
129	// Tick re-evaluates liveness against the clock. An Up path with no heartbeat for
130	// longer than DownAfter transitions Down; a Down path that has gone silent past
131	// DownAfter has its partial up-streak reset, so recovery requires a fresh run of
132	// UpAfterSuccesses consecutive echoes rather than echoes accumulated before the
133	// silence. Call Tick at least as often as the probe interval so detection latency
134	// stays within DownAfter plus one interval.
135	func (l *Liveness) Tick() {
136		now := l.clock.Now()
137		if !l.haveGood {
138			return
139		}
140		stale := now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter
141		switch {
142		case l.state == StateUp && stale:
143			l.goodStreak = 0
144			l.transition(StateDown, now)
145		case l.state == StateDown && stale:
146			l.goodStreak = 0
147		}
148	}
149	
150	// State returns the current liveness verdict.
151	func (l *Liveness) State() PathState { return l.state }
152	
153	func (l *Liveness) transition(to PathState, now time.Time) {
154		from := l.state
155		l.state = to
156		l.log.Info("path liveness transition",
157			"from", from.String(),
158			"to", to.String(),
159			"silence_ms", now.Sub(l.lastGood).Milliseconds(),
160		)
161	}
```

```go
10	// Field key conventions. Per-path events carry FieldPath so logs can be filtered
11	// by uplink; every component-scoped logger carries FieldComponent.
12	const (
13		FieldComponent = "component"
14		FieldPath      = "path"
15	)

17	// Logger is the structured-logging surface the rest of wanbond depends on, so no
18	// package imports log/slog directly. It supports component- and path-scoped
19	// child loggers whose scope attributes are attached to every subsequent record.
20	type Logger interface {
21		Debug(msg string, args ...any)
22		Info(msg string, args ...any)
23		Warn(msg string, args ...any)
24		Error(msg string, args ...any)
25		// Component returns a child logger tagged with the given component name.

29	}

31	// slogLogger is the slog-backed implementation of Logger.
32	type slogLogger struct {
33		l *slog.Logger
34	}

36	// New builds a JSON-handler Logger writing to w at the given level. An unknown
37	// level is a configuration error (fail fast); an empty level defaults to info.
38	func New(level string, w io.Writer) (Logger, error) {
39		lvl, err := parseLevel(level)
40		if err != nil {
41			return nil, err
42		}
43		h := slog.NewJSONHandler(w, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: lvl})
44		return &slogLogger{l: slog.New(h)}, nil
45	}
46	
47	func parseLevel(level string) (slog.Level, error) {
48		switch strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(level)) {
49		case "", "info":
50			return slog.LevelInfo, nil
51		case "debug":
52			return slog.LevelDebug, nil
53		case "warn", "warning":
54			return slog.LevelWarn, nil
55		case "error":
56			return slog.LevelError, nil
57		default:
58			return 0, fmt.Errorf("log: unknown level %q (want debug|info|warn|error)", level)
59		}
60	}
61	
62	func (s *slogLogger) Debug(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Debug(msg, args...) }
63	func (s *slogLogger) Info(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Info(msg, args...) }
64	func (s *slogLogger) Warn(msg string, args ...any)  { s.l.Warn(msg, args...) }
65	func (s *slogLogger) Error(msg string, args ...any) { s.l.Error(msg, args...) }
66	
67	func (s *slogLogger) Component(name string) Logger {
68		return &slogLogger{l: s.l.With(FieldComponent, name)}
69	}
70	
71	func (s *slogLogger) Path(name string) Logger {
```

```go
1	package telemetry
2	
3	import (
4		"time"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
7	)
8	
9	// Clock is the injectable time source the liveness machinery reads. Production
10	// code passes SystemClock; tests pass a hand-advanced fake so transitions are
11	// deterministic and instant.
12	type Clock interface {
13		Now() time.Time
14	}
15	
16	// SystemClock is the wall-clock Clock.
17	type SystemClock struct{}
18	
19	// Now returns the current wall-clock time.
20	func (SystemClock) Now() time.Time { return time.Now() }
21	
22	// PathState is the liveness verdict for one path. Path liveness is entirely this
23	// codec's concern: the inner WireGuard keepalive is per-peer, not per-path, so a
24	// dead uplink under a live peer is invisible to WireGuard and must be detected
25	// here.
26	type PathState uint8
27	
28	const (
29		// StateDown means no fresh probe echo has been observed within the detection
30		// threshold; the path is presumed unusable.
31		StateDown PathState = iota
32		// StateUp means the path has recently returned probe echoes.
33		StateUp
34	)
35	
36	// String renders the state as the lowercase token used in structured logs.
37	func (s PathState) String() string {
38		switch s {
39		case StateUp:
40			return "up"
41		case StateDown:
42			return "down"
43		default:
44			return "unknown"
45		}
46	}
47	
48	// LivenessConfig holds the up/down detection thresholds. DownAfter is the
49	// silence duration that marks an up path down; UpAfterSuccesses is the number of
50	// consecutive echoes required to bring a down path up. The asymmetry is the
51	// hysteresis: a single lost echo never flaps the path, and a single recovered
52	// echo never prematurely declares it healthy.
53	type LivenessConfig struct {
54		DownAfter        time.Duration
55		UpAfterSuccesses int
56	}
57	
58	// Default per-path liveness detection timing (T13/T37/T39). These are the SINGLE
59	// SOURCE OF TRUTH for probe cadence and up/down hysteresis. The daemon
60	// (internal/device) builds its ProberConfig from them and runs its probe loop at
61	// DefaultProbeInterval; the e2e acceptance table (test/e2e/thresholds.go) derives
62	// its PLiveness* budget from them. Neither can silently diverge from the timing the
63	// daemon actually runs, which is the D16 reconciliation: one place to retune.
64	//
65	//   - DefaultDownAfter is the silence that marks an UP path DOWN.
66	//   - DefaultProbeInterval is the probe emission AND liveness Tick cadence; detection
67	//     latency is therefore bounded by DownAfter + one interval.
68	//   - DefaultUpSuccesses is the consecutive echoes that bring a DOWN path UP.
69	//   - DefaultLossWindow (0) takes the estimator's default trailing window.
70	//
71	// DownAfter spans DefaultProbeInterval*6, so SIX consecutive lost echoes are needed
72	// before a false DOWN — the D13 jitter-safety margin. T39 tightened the pair from
73	// 1500ms/250ms to 1200ms/200ms to WIDEN the P1 3s-recovery margin while KEEPING
74	// that six-lost-echo false-down tolerance exactly (the ratio is unchanged). Detection
75	// latency drops from ~1.75s to ~1.4s worst case, and the receive-path liveness sweep
76	// (bind, D15) makes that detection robust to probe-loop-ticker starvation under load.
77	const (
78		DefaultProbeInterval = 200 * time.Millisecond
79		DefaultDownAfter     = 1200 * time.Millisecond
80		DefaultUpSuccesses   = 3
81		DefaultLossWindow    = 0
82	)
83	
84	// Liveness is the per-path up/down state machine. It is driven by two events:
85	// RecordEcho on every authenticated probe echo, and Tick on a periodic timer.
86	// It starts Down and requires UpAfterSuccesses echoes before declaring Up, so a
87	// path is never reported healthy before it has actually answered.
88	type Liveness struct {
89		cfg   LivenessConfig
90		clock Clock
91		log   log.Logger
92	
93		state      PathState
94		lastGood   time.Time
95		haveGood   bool
96		goodStreak int
97	}
98	
99	// NewLiveness builds a per-path liveness machine. The supplied logger is tagged
100	// with the path name so every transition record carries the per-path field.
101	func NewLiveness(pathName string, cfg LivenessConfig, clock Clock, logger log.Logger) *Liveness {
102		return &Liveness{
103			cfg:   cfg,
104			clock: clock,
105			log:   logger.Path(pathName),
106			state: StateDown,
107		}
108	}
109	
110	// RecordEcho registers a successful, authenticated probe echo as a liveness
111	// heartbeat. Only echoes that are CONSECUTIVE — no more than DownAfter apart —
112	// accumulate toward Up; an echo that arrives after a longer silence starts the
113	// streak over, so UpAfterSuccesses stray echoes spread across arbitrary silence
114	// never bring a Down path Up. Once UpAfterSuccesses consecutive heartbeats
115	// accumulate, a Down path transitions Up.
116	func (l *Liveness) RecordEcho() {
117		now := l.clock.Now()
118		if l.haveGood && now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter {
119			l.goodStreak = 0 // silence broke the run of consecutive echoes
120		}
121		l.lastGood = now
122		l.haveGood = true
123		l.goodStreak++
124		if l.state == StateDown && l.goodStreak >= l.cfg.UpAfterSuccesses {
125			l.transition(StateUp, now)
126		}
127	}
128	
129	// Tick re-evaluates liveness against the clock. An Up path with no heartbeat for
130	// longer than DownAfter transitions Down; a Down path that has gone silent past
131	// DownAfter has its partial up-streak reset, so recovery requires a fresh run of
132	// UpAfterSuccesses consecutive echoes rather than echoes accumulated before the
133	// silence. Call Tick at least as often as the probe interval so detection latency
134	// stays within DownAfter plus one interval.
135	func (l *Liveness) Tick() {
136		now := l.clock.Now()
137		if !l.haveGood {
138			return
139		}
140		stale := now.Sub(l.lastGood) > l.cfg.DownAfter
141		switch {
142		case l.state == StateUp && stale:
143			l.goodStreak = 0
144			l.transition(StateDown, now)
145		case l.state == StateDown && stale:
146			l.goodStreak = 0
147		}
148	}
149	
150	// State returns the current liveness verdict.
151	func (l *Liveness) State() PathState { return l.state }
152	
153	func (l *Liveness) transition(to PathState, now time.Time) {
154		from := l.state
155		l.state = to
156		l.log.Info("path liveness transition",
157			"from", from.String(),
158			"to", to.String(),
159			"silence_ms", now.Sub(l.lastGood).Milliseconds(),
160		)
161	}
```

1	//go:build e2e
2	
3	package e2e
4	
5	import (
6		"fmt"
7		"path/filepath"
8		"strconv"
9		"strings"
10		"testing"
11		"time"
12	
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
14	)
15	
16	// T104 (Q39, goals:G6) is an IN-GOAL VERIFICATION task, not a refactor: it asks
17	// whether an idle-but-"up" standby path's liveness is genuinely BIDIRECTIONAL, or
18	// whether it can be satisfied by receive-only traffic. The motivating production
19	// observation was wanbond_path_up{path="5g"}=1 with wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total{
20	// path="5g"}=0 — a standby path reported healthy while its tx counter never moved.
21	// Two independent checks:
22	//
23	//   - standby-transmits-when-idle: an UP standby that carries no DATA (active-backup
24	//     collapses all DATA onto the primary) must still be observed TRANSMITTING —
25	//     its own periodic liveness probes are real wire writes (bind.emitProbes ->
26	//     conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort) — while the primary carries a live flow.
27	//   - standby-egress-blocked-goes-down: with the standby's EGRESS direction (only)
28	//     blocked one-way at the edge veth (BlockEgress, a tc clsact/matchall/drop
29	//     filter — see netns.go), the standby must transition DOWN and must NOT be
30	//     selected when the primary is then killed. This is the affirmative half: it
31	//     proves the liveness verdict requires THIS path's own send-probe/receive-echo
32	//     round trip, not merely "traffic arrived on this interface" (the peer's own
33	//     probes keep ARRIVING at the blocked path the whole time — see
34	//     internal/bind/multipath.go dispatchInbound: an unechoed inbound PROBE only
35	//     learns the remote and is reflected; it never marks OUR liveness up — only
36	//     HandleEcho, driven by OUR OWN probe's authenticated echo, can).
37	//
38	// This harness cannot execute the `-tags e2e` netns tier itself (it requires
39	// CAP_NET_ADMIN/root and runs via the dedicated privileged target — see AGENTS.md);
40	// it is written to COMPILE and to make BOTH outcomes (pass, or a documented failure
41	// kept as a defect repro per Q39) observable once run there. A static read of the
42	// send path (internal/bind/probe.go's emitProbes never calls ps.txBytes.Add — only
43	// Send() and fecFlushDeadline() in internal/bind/multipath.go do) predicts the first
44	// subtest fails against the current implementation, reproducing the production
45	// observation; that is an inference from source, not a measured result — the
46	// authoritative answer is the hardware run's PASS/FAIL, at which point a genuine
47	// failure should be refiled as a defect linked to goals:G6 with this test kept as
48	// the repro, per the Q39 either-outcome acceptance.
49	const (
50		t104MetricsListen = "127.0.0.1:9100"
51		t104MetricsURL    = "http://" + t104MetricsListen + "/metrics"
52	
53		// t104ProbeWindow is the idle-standby observation window for the tx-growth
54		// check: long enough (15 probe intervals at the default 200ms cadence) that
55		// even ONE counted probe would show up as a nonzero delta, well clear of
56		// scrape/scheduling jitter.
57		t104ProbeWindow = 15 * PLivenessProbeInterval
58	)
59	
60	// TestStandbyLivenessBidirectional runs both T104 checks. Each brings up its own
61	// topology (sequentially — the fixture's fixed veth names forbid two live
62	// topologies), mirroring the subtest structure of TestP2Aggregation.
63	func TestStandbyLivenessBidirectional(t *testing.T) {
64		bin := buildWanbond(t)
65	
66		t.Run("standby-transmits-when-idle", func(t *testing.T) {
67			testStandbyIdleTransmits(t, bin)
68		})
69		t.Run("standby-egress-blocked-goes-down", func(t *testing.T) {
70			testStandbyEgressBlockedGoesDown(t, bin)
71		})
72	}
73	
74	// testStandbyIdleTransmits is the FIRST T104 check: with the primary (starlink)
75	// carrying a live flow and the standby (cellular) carrying no DATA (active-backup
76	// default), the standby's wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total must still grow over a
77	// multi-probe-interval window — its own periodic liveness probes are genuine wire
78	// writes even though no DATA rides them.
79	func testStandbyIdleTransmits(t *testing.T, bin string) {
80		t.Helper()
81		top := Setup(t)
82		edge, conc := setupT104Tunnel(t, top, bin, DefaultPaths, "error")
83	
84		if !top.pingUntil(concInner, 15*time.Second) {
85			t.Fatalf("bond never came up\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", edge.log(), conc.log())
86		}
87	
88		primary := DefaultPaths[primaryPathIdx] // starlink
89		standby := DefaultPaths[backupPathIdx]  // cellular
90	
91		waitPathUp(t, t104MetricsURL, standby.name, 1, 10*time.Second)
92	
93		// Drive a live flow on the primary for (at least) the whole observation window;
94		// active-backup keeps ALL of it off the standby, so the standby's own tx (if
95		// any is counted) can only come from its periodic probes.
96		loadSecs := int(t104ProbeWindow.Seconds()) + 10
97		top.startProc(t, "iperf3-server", "nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", "iperf3", "-s", "-1", "-B", concInner)
98		time.Sleep(400 * time.Millisecond)
99		top.startProc(t, "iperf3-load", "iperf3", "-c", concInner, "-t", strconv.Itoa(loadSecs))
100		time.Sleep(500 * time.Millisecond) // let the flow ramp before sampling the window
101	
102		before := scrapeMetrics(t, t104MetricsURL)
103		txBefore, ok := before.PathValue(metrics.MetricTxBytes, standby.name)
104		if !ok {
105			t.Fatalf("edge /metrics missing %s{path=%q}", metrics.MetricTxBytes, standby.name)
106		}
107		if up, ok := before.PathValue(metrics.MetricUp, standby.name); !ok || up != 1 {
108			t.Fatalf("standby %q not up at window start (up=%v ok=%v)\n%s", standby.name, up, ok, edge.log())
109		}
110		if up, ok := before.PathValue(metrics.MetricUp, primary.name); !ok || up != 1 {
111			t.Fatalf("primary %q not up at window start (up=%v ok=%v)\n%s", primary.name, up, ok, edge.log())
112		}
113	
114		time.Sleep(t104ProbeWindow)
115	
116		after := scrapeMetrics(t, t104MetricsURL)
117		txAfter, ok := after.PathValue(metrics.MetricTxBytes, standby.name)
118		if !ok {
119			t.Fatalf("edge /metrics missing %s{path=%q} on the second scrape", metrics.MetricTxBytes, standby.name)
120		}
121		if up, ok := after.PathValue(metrics.MetricUp, standby.name); !ok || up != 1 {
122			t.Fatalf("standby %q dropped from up during the idle window (up=%v ok=%v) — cannot judge idle-transmit liveness while down\n%s",
123				standby.name, up, ok, edge.log())
124		}
125	
126		delta := txAfter - txBefore
127		t.Logf("standby %q: wanbond_path_up=1 throughout a %s window with the primary carrying a live flow, tx delta = %.0f bytes",
128			standby.name, t104ProbeWindow, delta)
129		if delta <= 0 {
130			t.Errorf("standby path %q stayed wanbond_path_up=1 for the whole %s window while the primary carried a live "+
131				"flow, but its %s did not grow (delta=%.0f bytes) — this reproduces the production observation "+
132				"path_up{%s}=1 with tx{%s}=0. The standby's own periodic liveness probes ARE written to the wire "+
133				"(internal/bind/probe.go emitProbes -> conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort), but that write never increments "+
134				"ps.txBytes — only Send()/fecFlushDeadline() DATA/PARITY writes do (internal/bind/multipath.go). The "+
135				"path genuinely transmits; the operator-facing tx_bytes series just does not reflect it. Refile as a "+
136				"defect linked to goals:G6 (Q39) and keep this test as the reproduction.",
137				standby.name, t104ProbeWindow, metrics.MetricTxBytes, delta, standby.name, standby.name)
138		}
139	}
140	
141	// testStandbyEgressBlockedGoesDown is the SECOND T104 check: with the standby's
142	// egress (only) blocked one-way, it must transition DOWN — proving liveness needs
143	// this path's own probe/echo round trip, not merely inbound traffic arriving on it
144	// — and, once the primary is then killed, it must NOT be selected for failover.
145	func testStandbyEgressBlockedGoesDown(t *testing.T, bin string) {
146		t.Helper()
147		top := Setup(t)
148		// "info" so the scheduler's "active path change" transitions are readable from
149		// the edge log, the same idiom TestP1Failover uses.
150		edge, conc := setupT104Tunnel(t, top, bin, DefaultPaths, "info")
151	
152		if !top.pingUntil(concInner, 15*time.Second) {
153			t.Fatalf("bond never came up\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", edge.log(), conc.log())
154		}
155	
156		primary := DefaultPaths[primaryPathIdx] // starlink
157		standby := DefaultPaths[backupPathIdx]  // cellular
158	
159		waitPathUp(t, t104MetricsURL, standby.name, 1, 10*time.Second)
160	
161		// Block ONLY the standby's egress at the edge: outbound frames on its veth are
162		// dropped while the veth stays up and inbound (the concentrator's own probes)
163		// keeps arriving. A receive-only liveness check would stay wrongly up here.
164		top.BlockEgress(standby.name)
165		t.Cleanup(func() { top.UnblockEgress(standby.name) })
166	
167		waitPathUp(t, t104MetricsURL, standby.name, 0, PLivenessDetectBudget)
168		t.Logf("standby %q went DOWN within %s of its egress being blocked one-way (its own probe/echo round trip broke; inbound traffic alone did not keep it up)",
169			standby.name, PLivenessDetectBudget)
170	
171		// Force a failover decision: kill the still-healthy primary. If the standby's
172		// DOWN verdict is genuine, active-backup has no healthy path left, so egress
173		// must NOT reroute onto the standby and connectivity must NOT recover.
174		top.Blackhole(primary.name)
175		t.Cleanup(func() { top.Restore(primary.name) })
176	
177		noFailoverWindow := PLivenessFailoverBudget + 3*time.Second
178		if top.pingUntil(concInner, noFailoverWindow) {
179			t.Errorf("connectivity recovered within %s after the primary died with the standby's egress still blocked "+
180				"— the falsely-live standby was selected for failover\n--- edge ---\n%s", noFailoverWindow, edge.log())
181		} else {
182			t.Logf("connectivity correctly did NOT recover within %s — the down standby was not selected", noFailoverWindow)
183		}
184		if idx := currentActivePathIdx(edge.log()); idx == backupPathIdx {
185			t.Errorf("edge scheduler switched its active path to the standby (index %d, %q) despite its egress being "+
186				"blocked and its liveness DOWN\n%s", backupPathIdx, standby.name, edge.log())
187		}
188	
189		// Sanity: the fixture recovers once both faults are cleared — a genuine
190		// liveness-driven exclusion, not a wedged/broken harness.
191		top.UnblockEgress(standby.name)
192		top.Restore(primary.name)
193		if !top.pingUntil(concInner, 15*time.Second) {
194			t.Fatalf("bond did not recover after clearing the egress block and restoring the primary\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
195				edge.log(), conc.log())
196		}
197	}
198	
199	// setupT104Tunnel brings the multipath tunnel up over paths with the (default,
200	// omitted) active-backup scheduler and the /metrics endpoint enabled on the edge —
201	// only the edge's tx/up series are asserted by either T104 check, so only the edge
202	// carries the [metrics] block. It otherwise mirrors setupMultipathTunnelLevel.
203	func setupT104Tunnel(t *testing.T, top *Topology, bin string, paths []pathSpec, level string) (edge, conc *proc) {
204		t.Helper()
205	
206		edgePriv, edgePub := genKey(t)
207		concPriv, concPub := genKey(t)
208		psk := randKey(t)
209	
210		var edgePaths, concPaths strings.Builder
211		for _, p := range paths {
212			fmt.Fprintf(&edgePaths, "[[paths]]\nname = %q\nsource_addr = %q\ndest_addr = \"%s:%d\"\n\n", p.name, p.edgeIP, p.concIP, listenPort)
213			fmt.Fprintf(&concPaths, "[[paths]]\nname = %q\nsource_addr = %q\n\n", p.name, p.concIP)
214		}
215		primary := paths[0]
216		metricsBlock := fmt.Sprintf("[metrics]\nlisten = %q\n\n", t104MetricsListen)
217	
218		dir := t.TempDir()
219		edgeCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "edge.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
220	psk = "%s"
221	
222	%s%s[wireguard]
223	private_key = "%s"
224	
225	[[wireguard.peers]]
226	public_key = "%s"
227	endpoint = "%s:%d"
228	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
229	
230	[log]
231	level = %q
232	`, psk, edgePaths.String(), metricsBlock, edgePriv, concPub, primary.concIP, listenPort, concInner, level))
233	
234		concCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "conc.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
235	psk = "%s"
236	
237	%s[wireguard]
238	private_key = "%s"
239	listen_port = %d
240	
241	[[wireguard.peers]]
242	public_key = "%s"
243	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
244	
245	[log]
246	level = %q
247	`, psk, concPaths.String(), concPriv, listenPort, edgePub, edgeInner, level))
248	
249		conc = top.startProc(t, "concentrator", "nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", bin, "--config", concCfg)
250		edge = top.startProc(t, "edge", bin, "--config", edgeCfg)
251	
252		if !top.waitLink(tunDev, false, 5*time.Second) {
253			t.Fatalf("edge %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, edge.log())
254		}
255		if !top.waitLink(tunDev, true, 5*time.Second) {
256			t.Fatalf("concentrator %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, conc.log())
257		}
258		top.run("ip", "addr", "add", edgeInner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
259		top.run("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")
260		top.nsenter("ip", "addr", "add", concInner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
261		top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")
262		return edge, conc
263	}
264

45	)
46	
47	// Per-path metric names, exported so tests and future e2e harnesses can assert
48	// series by name without restating the FQ-name construction.
49	const (
50		MetricTxBytes    = "wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total"
51		MetricRxBytes    = "wanbond_path_rx_bytes_total"
52		MetricLoss       = "wanbond_path_loss_ratio"
53		MetricRTT        = "wanbond_path_rtt_seconds"
54		MetricJitter     = "wanbond_path_jitter_seconds"
55		MetricThroughput = "wanbond_path_throughput_bits_per_second"
56		MetricUp         = "wanbond_path_up"
57	)
58	
59	// FEC metric names. These connection-scoped series (no path label — FEC
60	// repair/recovery is per-connection, not per-uplink) are populated from the live FEC
61	// plane (T24): repair = parity frames emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead), recovered =
62	// data frames reconstructed from parity, unrecoverable = data frames lost beyond
63	// repair capacity.
64	const (
65		MetricFECData          = "wanbond_fec_data_packets_total"
66		MetricFECRepair        = "wanbond_fec_repair_packets_total"
67		MetricFECRecovered     = "wanbond_fec_recovered_packets_total"
68		MetricFECUnrecoverable = "wanbond_fec_unrecoverable_packets_total"
69		// Byte-denominated FEC overhead (T29). The adaptive-vs-fixed overhead comparison the
70		// P4 acceptance makes is in BYTES (parity shards are max-shard-sized while DATA frames
71		// vary), so these expose the byte numerator/denominator the frame counters above cannot:
72		// overhead = repair_bytes / data_bytes.
73		MetricFECDataBytes   = "wanbond_fec_data_bytes_total"
74		MetricFECRepairBytes = "wanbond_fec_repair_bytes_total"
75		// MetricFECResidualLoss is the post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction (T29): the
76		// share of outer-seqs neither natively received nor reconstructed from parity — the loss
77		// FEC did not mask. It is the P4 residual-loss acceptance signal.
78		MetricFECResidualLoss = "wanbond_fec_residual_loss_ratio"
79	)
80	
81	// Resequencer metric names (T94). Like the FEC series, these are per-PEER (a peer's
82	// resequencer buffers its whole bonded stream, not one uplink), so they carry no path
83	// label — only the conditional peer label. Sourced verbatim from reseq.Stats (see
84	// ReseqSnapshot); see reseq.Stats' field comments for each counter's exact meaning.
85	const (
86		MetricReseqReleased       = "wanbond_resequencer_released_frames_total"
87		MetricReseqDroppedDup     = "wanbond_resequencer_dropped_duplicate_frames_total"
88		MetricReseqDroppedOld     = "wanbond_resequencer_dropped_stale_frames_total"
89		MetricReseqDroppedSuspect = "wanbond_resequencer_dropped_suspect_frames_total"
90		MetricReseqSkipped        = "wanbond_resequencer_skipped_seqs_total"
91		MetricReseqResyncs        = "wanbond_resequencer_resyncs_total"
92		MetricReseqRebaselines    = "wanbond_resequencer_rebaselines_total"
93	)
94	
95	// WG-session metric names (I2). These connection-scoped series (no path label — the WG
96	// session is per-connection, not per-uplink) expose whether the amneziawg engine has a
97	// live session and how stale its last handshake is. Together they distinguish a tunnel
98	// that is STILL CONVERGING (established = 0, no completed handshake) from one that is
99	// WEDGED (a path is up but the handshake is absent or has aged out) — the signal
100	// D35/D36/D37 all presented identically without.
101	const (
102		MetricSessionEstablished   = "wanbond_session_established"
103		MetricSessionLastHandshake = "wanbond_session_last_handshake_seconds"
104	)
105	
106	// FECSnapshot is the current connection-scoped FEC signal set the exposition layer
107	// reports (T24). It is sourced from the multipath Bind's FEC counters, read at scrape
108	// time like the per-path snapshots. All zero when FEC is disabled.
109	type FECSnapshot struct {
110		// Peer attributes this snapshot to a bound peer (T94); see the package-level
111		// back-compat rule for when it surfaces as the `peer` label. "" on a Source with a
112		// single bound peer.
113		Peer string
114		// DataPackets is the cumulative count of DATA frames the FEC encoder emitted — the
115		// denominator of the fixed-ratio overhead (RepairPackets/DataPackets tends to M/K).
116		DataPackets uint64
117		// RepairPackets is the cumulative count of parity frames emitted — the fixed-ratio
118		// FEC overhead in packets.
119		RepairPackets uint64
120		// RecoveredPackets is the cumulative count of data frames reconstructed from parity.
121		RecoveredPackets uint64
122		// UnrecoverablePackets is the cumulative count of data frames lost beyond FEC repair
123		// capacity.
124		UnrecoverablePackets uint64
125		// DataBytes / RepairBytes are the cumulative DATA and parity frame WIRE bytes — the
126		// byte-denominated overhead numerator/denominator (T29): overhead = RepairBytes/DataBytes.
127		DataBytes   uint64
128		RepairBytes uint64
129		// ResidualLossRatio is the current post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1]
130		// (T29) — the loss FEC did not mask (the P4 acceptance signal). Zero when FEC is off.
131		ResidualLossRatio float64
132	}
133	
134	// PathSnapshot is the current per-path signal set the exposition layer reports.
135	// It fuses traffic accounting (TxBytes/RxBytes/Throughput, sourced from the bind
136	// and scheduler) with the telemetry plane's quality estimate and liveness verdict
137	// (Estimate/State, sourced verbatim from a Prober's Estimate()/State()). The
138	// metrics layer never measures these itself; it reads a Source at scrape time.
139	type PathSnapshot struct {
140		// Peer attributes this snapshot to a bound peer (T94); see the package-level
141		// back-compat rule for when it surfaces as the `peer` label. "" on a Source with a
142		// single bound peer.
143		Peer string
144		// Name is the stable path identifier used as the `path` label. It must be
145		// unique within one peer's entries of a single Source.Paths() result (T94: it need
146		// not be globally unique across peers — a per-(peer,path) pair is the true key, which
147		// is why the throughput last-sample map in internal/device/metrics.go keys on both).
148		Name string
149		// TxBytes and RxBytes are cumulative byte counters for the path.
150		TxBytes uint64
151		RxBytes uint64
152		// ThroughputBitsPerSecond is the path's current send+receive throughput.
153		ThroughputBitsPerSecond float64
154		// Estimate carries per-path RTT/jitter/loss, read verbatim from telemetry.
155		Estimate telemetry.Estimate
156		// State is the per-path liveness verdict, read verbatim from telemetry.
157		State telemetry.PathState
158	}
159	
160	// ReseqSnapshot is the current per-peer resequencer signal set the exposition layer
161	// reports (T94). It embeds reseq.Stats verbatim — mirroring how PathSnapshot embeds
162	// telemetry.Estimate/PathState fields — read at scrape time from the peer's
163	// resequencer with no local aggregation. Like FECSnapshot it is per-PEER, not
164	// per-path: a peer's resequencer buffers its whole bonded stream, not one uplink.
165	type ReseqSnapshot struct {
166		// Peer attributes this snapshot to a bound peer (T94); see the package-level
167		// back-compat rule for when it surfaces as the `peer` label. "" on a Source with a
168		// single bound peer.
169		Peer string
170		reseq.Stats
171	}
172	
173	// SessionSnapshot is the current WG-session signal set the exposition layer reports
174	// (I2). It is sourced at scrape time from the amneziawg engine's peer last-handshake
175	// state by the device layer; the bind stays WG-unaware. The device layer owns the
176	// freshness policy (whether a completed-but-aged handshake still counts as
177	// established), so the metrics layer merely exposes the resolved verdict and age.
178	type SessionSnapshot struct {
179		// Established is the current WG-session liveness verdict: true when a handshake has
180		// completed AND is still within the session-validity window (fresh). A tunnel that
181		// has never handshaked (still converging) or whose handshake has aged out (wedged)
182		// reports false.
183		Established bool
184		// LastHandshakeAge is the elapsed time since the peer's most recent completed
185		// handshake. It is zero when no handshake has ever completed (Established is then
186		// false); read together with Established it disambiguates "never handshaked"
187		// (Established=false, age=0) from "handshake aged out" (Established=false, age large).
188		LastHandshakeAge time.Duration
189	}
190	
191	// Source is the read-only seam between the traffic/telemetry planes and the
192	// exposition layer. The collector calls Paths/FEC/Reseq at every scrape, so an
193	// implementation must be safe for concurrent use and must return a consistent
194	// snapshot (unique (peer,path) names) cheaply — it is on the scrape hot path.

```go
1972		// encoder's per-group parity BEFORE flushing, so a group closed by this tick already

1974		// probe cadence. Runs under the same m.mu the flush holds — the single serialized FEC
1975		// locus — so the controller (a state machine) is never touched concurrently.
1976		m.driveAdaptiveControllerLocked()

1978		if err != nil || len(parity) == 0 {

1983		// Send). A shed/no-path verdict drops this group's parity — a degraded path is
1984		// exactly when the datapath is under pressure, and a stranded partial group's parity
1985		// is best-effort — so the group simply goes unprotected rather than blocking. FEC
1986		// parity is bulk redundancy, so it is paced as ClassData (defect D22): only WireGuard
1987		// control frames earn the pacing exemption.
1988		idx := m.scheduler.Pick(sched.ClassData)

1992		}

1994		remote, ok := ps.getRemote()

1996			m.mu.Unlock()
1997			return
1998		}

2000		wires := make([][]byte, 0, len(parity))
2001		for _, par := range parity {

2003			// m.paths reach the primary by promotion), so its parity is framed with the
2004			// primary's send Codec.
2005			pw, err := m.encodeParityLocked(m.peerState, par, ps.id)
2006			if err != nil {
2007				m.mu.Unlock()
2008				return
2009			}

2011		}
2012		m.mu.Unlock()

2019			fs.parityFrames.Add(1)
```

```go
13	// satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in the adapter's unit test, so the mapping and rate

16	// single-peer edge/hub still maps 1:1 onto today's series (len(PeerSnapshots())==1) and
17	// a multi-peer concentrator's additional peers surface as additional entries.

22	// sampleKey identifies one (peer,path) pair in the throughput last-sample map (T94): a
23	// path name alone is only unique WITHIN one peer, not across peers (each peer's path
24	// set is independently configured/named in principle), so keying by path name alone
25	// would let two peers' samples for a same-named path clobber each other's rate
26	// derivation. peer is "" for the single-peer edge/hub and a concentrator's primary,
27	// exactly matching PeerSnapshot.Name — so a single-peer config's map degenerates to the
28	// pre-T94 keying (peer is always "", so the key varies only by path).

30		peer, path string
31	}

33	// metricsSource adapts the multipath Bind onto metrics.Source: at every scrape it
34	// reads the Bind's cumulative per-(peer,path) byte counters + telemetry, and DERIVES

36	// the previous scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the
37	// Bind's hot path a pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the
38	// only stateful, scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a
39	// (peer,path) reports zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move
40	// backwards (only possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that
41	// interval rather than a spurious negative.
42	type metricsSource struct {
43		provider trafficProvider
44		// session yields the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2), read from the
45		// engine at scrape time. It is separate from provider because the bind stays
46		// WG-unaware — the session signal is sourced by the device layer's engine seam.
47		session sessionSnapshotter
48		clock   telemetry.Clock
49	
50		mu   sync.Mutex
51		last map[sampleKey]byteSample
52	}
53

55	// the instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.

57		total   uint64
58		atNanos int64
59	}
60

62	// and resequencer counters) and the engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The
63	// clock is injected so the throughput derivation is deterministic under test.

65		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[sampleKey]byteSample)}
66	}

68	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
69	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-
70	// (peer,path) last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PeerSnapshots() reads
71	// atomics and each prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock
72	// across a prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
73	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
75		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
76	
77		total := 0
78		for _, peer := range peers {
79			total += len(peer.Paths)
80		}
81	
82		s.mu.Lock()
83		defer s.mu.Unlock()
84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)
85		for _, peer := range peers {
86			for _, t := range peer.Paths {
87				key := sampleKey{peer: peer.Name, path: t.Name}
88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
89				var tput float64
90				if prev, ok := s.last[key]; ok {
91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}

113	// per-path throughput, the FEC counters are cumulative and need no rate derivation or
114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
115	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() []metrics.FECSnapshot {
116		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()

124				RecoveredPackets:     f.Recovered,
125				UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
126				DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
127				RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
128				ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
129			}
130		}

140		for i, peer := range peers {
141			out[i] = metrics.ReseqSnapshot{Peer: peer.Name, Stats: peer.Reseq}
142		}
143		return out
144	}

148	// collector's whole life (T94) — see the metrics package doc for the back-compat rule.
```

```go
14	
15	// tunDiagnosticInterval is the minimum spacing between actionable EIO diagnostics for a
16	// single TUN. An interface left DOWN (or otherwise wedged) fails EVERY subsequent write, so
17	// without a rate limit a write storm would emit one diagnostic record per dropped packet.
18	// The raw amneziawg-go engine error ("Failed to write packets to TUN device: input/output
19	// error", logged unconditionally via engineLogger's Errorf) still surfaces on every failure
20	// underneath — this decorator adds ONE extra, actionable record per interval on top of that,
21	// it never suppresses the engine's own logging.
22	const tunDiagnosticInterval = 30 * time.Second
23	
24	// diagnosingTUN wraps a tun.Device and, on a Write that fails with EIO, emits ONE rate-limited
25	// actionable ERROR naming the probable cause (the interface's link state and MTU) and the
26	// remedy, alongside the raw error for debugging (D39, I3). Left undiagnosed, a write to a DOWN
27	// wanbond0 surfaces only as the engine's generic "input/output error" — accurate but useless to
28	// an operator who has never hit the failure mode before. Every OTHER tun.Device method passes
29	// straight through via the embedded interface; only Write is intercepted.
30	type diagnosingTUN struct {
31		tun.Device
32		name string
33		log  log.Logger
34		// now and probeState are seams for tests: production wires time.Now and ifState (the real
35		// SIOCGIFFLAGS/SIOCGIFMTU inspection); a test injects a fake clock and a scripted interface
36		// state so it can assert the DOWN/UP wording without a real, privileged network interface.
37		now        func() time.Time
38		probeState func(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error)
39	
40		mu       sync.Mutex
41		lastDiag time.Time
42	}
43	
44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}
49	
50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}
60	
61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}
```

```go
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"sync"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
7		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
9	)
10	
11	// trafficProvider is the read seam the metrics adapter consumes: the multipath Bind's
12	// per-PEER traffic+telemetry, FEC, and resequencer snapshot (T93/T94). *bind.Multipath
13	// satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in the adapter's unit test, so the mapping and rate
14	// derivation are testable without a running engine. PeerSnapshots generalizes the
15	// pre-T94 PathSnapshots/FECSnapshot (primary-peer-only) to every bound peer, so a
16	// single-peer edge/hub still maps 1:1 onto today's series (len(PeerSnapshots())==1) and
17	// a multi-peer concentrator's additional peers surface as additional entries.
18	type trafficProvider interface {
19		PeerSnapshots() []bind.PeerSnapshot
20	}
21	
22	// sampleKey identifies one (peer,path) pair in the throughput last-sample map (T94): a
23	// path name alone is only unique WITHIN one peer, not across peers (each peer's path
24	// set is independently configured/named in principle), so keying by path name alone
25	// would let two peers' samples for a same-named path clobber each other's rate
26	// derivation. peer is "" for the single-peer edge/hub and a concentrator's primary,
27	// exactly matching PeerSnapshot.Name — so a single-peer config's map degenerates to the
28	// pre-T94 keying (peer is always "", so the key varies only by path).
29	type sampleKey struct {
30		peer, path string
31	}
32	
33	// metricsSource adapts the multipath Bind onto metrics.Source: at every scrape it
34	// reads the Bind's cumulative per-(peer,path) byte counters + telemetry, and DERIVES
35	// the per-(peer,path) throughput as the rate of the (tx+rx) byte-counter delta since
36	// the previous scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the
37	// Bind's hot path a pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the
38	// only stateful, scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a
39	// (peer,path) reports zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move
40	// backwards (only possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that
41	// interval rather than a spurious negative.
42	type metricsSource struct {
43		provider trafficProvider
44		// session yields the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot (I2), read from the
45		// engine at scrape time. It is separate from provider because the bind stays
46		// WG-unaware — the session signal is sourced by the device layer's engine seam.
47		session sessionSnapshotter
48		clock   telemetry.Clock
49	
50		mu   sync.Mutex
51		last map[sampleKey]byteSample
52	}
53	
54	// byteSample is the previous scrape's cumulative (tx+rx) total for one (peer,path) and
55	// the instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.
56	type byteSample struct {
57		total   uint64
58		atNanos int64
59	}
60	
61	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind (per-peer path traffic, FEC,
62	// and resequencer counters) and the engine session seam (WG-session snapshot). The
63	// clock is injected so the throughput derivation is deterministic under test.
64	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
65		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[sampleKey]byteSample)}
66	}
67	
68	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
69	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-
70	// (peer,path) last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PeerSnapshots() reads
71	// atomics and each prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock
72	// across a prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
73	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
74		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
75		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
76	
77		total := 0
78		for _, peer := range peers {
79			total += len(peer.Paths)
80		}
81	
82		s.mu.Lock()
83		defer s.mu.Unlock()
84		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, 0, total)
85		for _, peer := range peers {
86			for _, t := range peer.Paths {
87				key := sampleKey{peer: peer.Name, path: t.Name}
88				byteTotal := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
89				var tput float64
90				if prev, ok := s.last[key]; ok {
91					dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
92					if dtSeconds > 0 && byteTotal >= prev.total {
93						tput = float64(byteTotal-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
94					}
95				}
96				s.last[key] = byteSample{total: byteTotal, atNanos: nowNanos}
97				out = append(out, metrics.PathSnapshot{
98					Peer:                    peer.Name,
99					Name:                    t.Name,
100					TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
101					RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
102					ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
103					Estimate:                t.Estimate,
104					State:                   t.State,
105				})
106			}
107		}
108		return out
109	}
110	
111	// FEC implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer connection-scoped FEC
112	// counters (T24, T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer. Unlike the
113	// per-path throughput, the FEC counters are cumulative and need no rate derivation or
114	// prior-sample state, so this is a direct pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
115	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() []metrics.FECSnapshot {
116		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
117		out := make([]metrics.FECSnapshot, len(peers))
118		for i, peer := range peers {
119			f := peer.FEC
120			out[i] = metrics.FECSnapshot{
121				Peer:                 peer.Name,
122				DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,
123				RepairPackets:        f.ParityFrames,
124				RecoveredPackets:     f.Recovered,
125				UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
126				DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
127				RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
128				ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
129			}
130		}
131		return out
132	}
133	
134	// Reseq implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's per-peer resequencer counters
135	// (T94) verbatim at scrape time, one entry per bound peer — a direct pass-through of
136	// the resequencer's own cumulative Stats(), like FEC needing no rate derivation.
137	func (s *metricsSource) Reseq() []metrics.ReseqSnapshot {
138		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
139		out := make([]metrics.ReseqSnapshot, len(peers))
140		for i, peer := range peers {
141			out[i] = metrics.ReseqSnapshot{Peer: peer.Name, Stats: peer.Reseq}
142		}
143		return out
144	}
145	
146	// PeerNames implements metrics.Source: it returns the current bound-peer name set,
147	// queried once by metrics.NewCollector to fix the `peer` label's presence for the
148	// collector's whole life (T94) — see the metrics package doc for the back-compat rule.
149	func (s *metricsSource) PeerNames() []string {
150		peers := s.provider.PeerSnapshots()
151		names := make([]string, len(peers))
152		for i, peer := range peers {
153			names[i] = peer.Name
154		}
155		return names
156	}
157	
158	// Session implements metrics.Source: it reads the connection-scoped WG-session snapshot
159	// (I2) from the engine seam at scrape time. Like FEC it is a direct pass-through of a
160	// snapshot the underlying monitor computes on demand — no per-scrape state is kept here
161	// (the 0->1 edge log is driven by the separate session-monitor poll loop, not by scrapes).
162	func (s *metricsSource) Session() metrics.SessionSnapshot {
163		return s.session.SessionSnapshot()
164	}
```

**`internal/device/tundiag.go`** — Write(method), diagnosingTUN(struct), diagnoseEIO(method), newDiagnosingTUN(function), tunDiagnosticInterval(constant)

```go
1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"fmt"
6		"sync"
7		"syscall"
8		"time"
9	
10		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
11	
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13	)
14	
15	// tunDiagnosticInterval is the minimum spacing between actionable EIO diagnostics for a
16	// single TUN. An interface left DOWN (or otherwise wedged) fails EVERY subsequent write, so
17	// without a rate limit a write storm would emit one diagnostic record per dropped packet.
18	// The raw amneziawg-go engine error ("Failed to write packets to TUN device: input/output
19	// error", logged unconditionally via engineLogger's Errorf) still surfaces on every failure
20	// underneath — this decorator adds ONE extra, actionable record per interval on top of that,
21	// it never suppresses the engine's own logging.
22	const tunDiagnosticInterval = 30 * time.Second
23	
24	// diagnosingTUN wraps a tun.Device and, on a Write that fails with EIO, emits ONE rate-limited
25	// actionable ERROR naming the probable cause (the interface's link state and MTU) and the
26	// remedy, alongside the raw error for debugging (D39, I3). Left undiagnosed, a write to a DOWN
27	// wanbond0 surfaces only as the engine's generic "input/output error" — accurate but useless to
28	// an operator who has never hit the failure mode before. Every OTHER tun.Device method passes
29	// straight through via the embedded interface; only Write is intercepted.
30	type diagnosingTUN struct {
31		tun.Device
32		name string
33		log  log.Logger
34		// now and probeState are seams for tests: production wires time.Now and ifState (the real
35		// SIOCGIFFLAGS/SIOCGIFMTU inspection); a test injects a fake clock and a scripted interface
36		// state so it can assert the DOWN/UP wording without a real, privileged network interface.
37		now        func() time.Time
38		probeState func(name string) (up bool, mtu int, err error)
39	
40		mu       sync.Mutex
41		lastDiag time.Time
42	}
43	
44	// newDiagnosingTUN wraps dev for production use: real wall-clock rate-limiting and the real
45	// (platform-specific) interface-state inspection.
46	func newDiagnosingTUN(dev tun.Device, name string, lg log.Logger) *diagnosingTUN {
47		return &diagnosingTUN{Device: dev, name: name, log: lg, now: time.Now, probeState: ifState}
48	}
49	
50	// Write delegates to the wrapped Device and, on an EIO failure, reports a rate-limited
51	// actionable diagnostic. The original error is returned UNCHANGED: this decorator only adds a
52	// diagnostic side effect, it never masks or alters the engine's own error handling.
53	func (d *diagnosingTUN) Write(bufs [][]byte, offset int) (int, error) {
54		n, err := d.Device.Write(bufs, offset)
55		if err != nil && errors.Is(err, syscall.EIO) {
56			d.diagnoseEIO(err)
57		}
58		return n, err
59	}
60	
61	// diagnoseEIO inspects the interface state and logs ONE actionable ERROR record, then
62	// suppresses further diagnostics for tunDiagnosticInterval so a write storm (every dropped
63	// packet failing until the operator acts) produces one record, not a flood.
64	func (d *diagnosingTUN) diagnoseEIO(writeErr error) {
65		d.mu.Lock()
66		now := d.now()
67		if !d.lastDiag.IsZero() && now.Sub(d.lastDiag) < tunDiagnosticInterval {
68			d.mu.Unlock()
69			return
70		}
71		d.lastDiag = now
72		d.mu.Unlock()
73	
74		var errno syscall.Errno
75		_ = errors.As(writeErr, &errno)
76	
77		up, mtu, stateErr := d.probeState(d.name)
78		switch {
79		case stateErr != nil:
80			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
81				"errno", int(errno), "state", "unknown",
82				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("could not inspect %s state (%v) — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name, stateErr))
83		case !up:
84			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
85				"errno", int(errno), "state", "DOWN", "mtu", mtu,
86				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is DOWN — address & bring it up (install.md §4)", d.name))
87		default:
88			d.log.Error("TUN write failed with I/O error", "interface", d.name, "raw_error", writeErr.Error(),
89				"errno", int(errno), "state", "UP", "mtu", mtu,
90				"hint", fmt.Sprintf("%s is UP (mtu=%d) but writes are still failing — check the link/driver (install.md §4)", d.name, mtu))
91		}
92	}
```
